bio.specialty: Oriental vegan shōjin ryori — recurring signature dishes mentioned across reviews include dandan (tantan) noodles, a vegetable-broth noodle soup, ramen, bibimbap, and a lion's mane mushroom dish
bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames shōjin ryori as drawing out the umami, nutritional value and beauty already present in each plant-based ingredient, with a stated ethos that nothing should be wasted — down to a single grain of rice or drop of juice.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer described the restaurant's vegetable-only noodle broth as having a surprisingly deep, umami-rich flavor close to a Kansai-style dashi despite containing no fish or bonito stock.
The restaurant has hosted seasonal, hands-on events such as an Oriental-vegan chimaki (rice dumpling) making experience, and prepared a fully vegan osechi (Japanese New Year) box for 2023.

caveats: Dinner service is reservation-only (contact by 3pm the previous day); lunch is walk-in. No page found with an explicit gluten-free protocol; menu includes wheat-based noodle dishes (tantan/ramen-style), so celiac-level GF safety cannot be confirmed — ask staff directly. neighborhood: Taito-ku, Asakusa (Hanakawado), directly by Asakusa Station North Exit, near Sensōji

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is described as excluding all animal-derived ingredients as well as the Buddhist "five pungent roots" (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion), consistent with strict shōjin-style plant-based cooking.

positives.text: Menu and site descriptions consistently state the food uses no animal products at all (fully vegan shōjin ryori), not just a vegetarian subset of dishes.
A reviewer noted the noodle-dish broth was vegetable-only yet tasted deeply umami, suggesting deliberate care in building flavor without fish-based dashi.

seats_text: Not published in any source found; likely a small restaurant given the reservation-only dinner policy, but exact capacity unconfirmed.
vegan_detail: Consistently described across multiple independent sources as a fully vegan shōjin-ryori kitchen: no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs, and no "five pungent" alliums (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion) per Buddhist dietary tradition. Broths appear to be vegetable-based rather than fish/bonito-based, per reviewer commentary on the noodle dishes. No katsuo/dashi exceptions were found in any source.

lead: 食事処 酒肴 浅草 水口

roles: family-run Showa-era teishoku-ya (set-meal diner) and neighborhood eatery/izakaya
bio.origin: Opened in 1950 (Shōwa 25) in Asakusa Rokku, Asakusa's old entertainment district, and has stayed in the same neighborhood ever since — marking roughly 75 years in business as of the mid-2020s.
bio.background: Mizuguchi began as a plain teishoku-ya serving the workers and performers of Asakusa Rokku in the postwar years. It has grown into a sprawling two-floor shokudō-and-izakaya known for its huge, old-fashioned menu spanning Japanese, Western and Chinese-style home cooking — everything from fried horse mackerel and pork sauté to omurice, ginger pork, mapo tofu and hand-mixed hamburg steak — served in a boisterous, nostalgic Shōwa-retro dining room.
bio.specialty: Iri-buta — pork and onion stir-fried in Mizuguchi's own curry-tinged, sweet-savory sauce, reportedly created together with one of the shop's cooks around the time of the Tokyo air raids of 1945 and still the table everyone orders.
bio.philosophy: A no-frills, high-volume local shokudō where regulars — from neighborhood workers to solo diners stopping in for a drink — are as much a part of the atmosphere as the food.

anecdotes.text: Iri-buta is said to have existed in some form before the war and to have been worked out with one of the shop's cooks around the time of the 1945 Tokyo air raids.
The comedy duo Naitsu (ナイツ), who often riff on Asakusa in their act, are described as regulars who frequent the shop.
Local write-ups describe the restaurant as a 100-plus-item menu institution beloved by Asakusa locals, with the Shōwa-retro dining room drawing return visitors.

caveats: Traditional Shōwa-era set-meal diner (teishoku-ya) with no gluten-free or vegan program of any kind identified in any source. Menu is dominated by pork, fried and breaded dishes, and Western-Japanese home cooking with conventional soy sauce and likely katsuo/bonito-based dashi. Not a fit for strict celiac or vegan diners; listed for completeness/honesty rather than as a recommended GF/vegan pick. Geocoding could not be confirmed via API in this session (network policy blocked msearch.gsi.go.jp, geocoding.jp and nominatim.openstreetmap.org), but the address (Asakusa 2-4-9, ~1 min from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, ~7 min from Ginza-line Asakusa/Tawaramachi) places it well within the Sensōji target radius.
closed_days: Wednesday
cuisine: 定食・いり豚・昭和食堂 (Shōwa-era set-meal diner / teishoku-ya)
cultural_comfort_note: A very local, Japanese-language diner with a huge handwritten-style menu; no English menu or allergy accommodations were found in any source, so visitors comfortable navigating in Japanese (or with a translation app) will have the easiest time.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. The menu is built around breaded/fried items (mixed fry, korokke), sauced pork and hamburg-steak dishes, and standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, so wheat exposure should be assumed throughout the kitchen. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
geocode_note: Could not geocode via the GSI address-search API (or fallback geocoders geocoding.jp / nominatim.openstreetmap.org) — all were blocked by this session's network egress policy (403 on every attempt). Coordinates left null rather than invented. The address (Asakusa 2-4-9, described in multiple sources as ~1 minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station and ~7 minutes from Ginza-line Asakusa/Tawaramachi stations) is consistent with being well inside the 0.8km Sensōji radius, but this was not independently confirmed with a plotted lat/lng.
name_ja: 食事処 酒肴 浅草 水口
seats_text: Two floors: roughly 36 table seats on the 1st floor and about 54 seats (tables and tatami) on the 2nd floor.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific dishes, marking, or accommodations were found in any source. The menu is heavily meat- and fish-based (pork specialties, hamburg steak, fried items, seafood), and traditional Japanese dashi (typically katsuobushi/bonito-based) should be assumed in soups and simmered dishes unless the restaurant confirms otherwise. Not recommended as a vegan option based on available information.

roles: Family-run since the Meiji era, now several generations on
bio.origin: Founded in the last years of the Edo period as a tempura food stall on Hirokoji street by Nakagawa Tetsuzō, a former samurai from Suruga who gave up his rank to start a business in Edo. In 1870 (Meiji 3) the family opened a permanent shop in front of the old Asakusa Public Hall, on the same ground it occupies today. The restaurant's name combines "中" from the Nakagawa family name with "清" from Seigorō, the second-generation owner.
bio.specialty: Raijin-age (雷神揚げ) — an oversized kakiage of shiba shrimp and akoyagi (pen shell) adductor muscle, fried crisp in sesame oil using a technique handed down within the family since the first generation. The name was coined by a regular customer, French-literature scholar Yutaka Tatsuno, who thought the finished kakiage resembled the thunder drums carried by the Raijin (Thunder God) statue at Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate.
bio.philosophy: A multi-generation commitment to Edo-mae tempura as it was cooked in old Tokyo — thick-battered, sesame-oil-fried, whole ingredients rather than fillets — with the signature kakiage recipe treated as a piece of family craft passed one generation to the next.

anecdotes.text: The shop appears repeatedly in the writings of novelist Nagai Kafū, and novelist Ikenami Shōtarō was also a regular visitor.
Writer Kubo Mantarō was a childhood friend of the third-generation owner, Tetsutarō, and used to play with him in the restaurant's courtyard.
The restaurant was used as a filming location for the TV drama 'Kurosagi'.

The 'Raijin-age' kakiage — shiba shrimp and akoyagi adductor fried in sesame oil — is described as a technique 'passed down to a single successor per generation' (一子相伝), originally devised by a former-samurai founder.

bio.background: An 1870-founded Edo-mae tempura house near Sensō-ji, run by descendants of its samurai-turned-restaurateur founder, known for its quiet courtyard-pond setting, sukiya-style private tatami rooms, and a kakiage recipe kept within the family for generations.

caveats: This is a historic, seafood-centric Edo-mae tempura restaurant (course-only, private tatami rooms, roughly ¥11,000 average spend). No gluten-free or vegan menu, ingredient substitution, or allergy program was found in any source consulted (official site, Tabelog, tourism association pages, food blogs). The batter, tentsuyu dipping sauce, and signature kakiage are all built around wheat and seafood/dashi, so celiac and vegan diners should treat this as an unsuitable choice or contact the restaurant directly (Japanese phone reservation, 03-3841-4015) before booking rather than assume any accommodation.
closed_days: Every Tuesday and Wednesday; also closed December 31 and January 1. The restaurant notes closure dates may change with circumstances.
cuisine: 天ぷら（Edo-mae tempura — sesame-oil-fried seafood and vegetables, whole-ingredient style）
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains an English-language section of its official site and is bookable through English-facing reservation platforms (e.g. Autoreserve, Last Minute Japan), but seating is private-room, course-only and reservation is effectively required, so a visitor with zero Japanese should book ahead through one of those English channels rather than walk in.
geocode_note: Could not geocode: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and other geocoding services (Nominatim, geocoding.jp) were unreachable in this environment (network egress policy blocked the connection, 403 on every attempt). The address was instead confirmed textually across multiple independent sources (search results referencing the official site, Tabelog, and the Asakusa tourism association) as 東京都台東区浅草1-39-13, in front of the former Asakusa Public Hall — a location consistently described as a 5-minute walk from Sensō-ji, consistent with the target area, but precise coordinates could not be verified.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, batter substitution, or dedicated frying process was found in any source. Traditional Edo-mae tempura batter is wheat-flour based, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce that accompanies it is standard dashi-and-soy-sauce (both typically containing wheat: soy sauce as an ingredient, bonito/kelp dashi as a shared base). No information confirms a dedicated gluten-free fryer or ingredient list, so this should be assumed unsafe for celiac diners unless confirmed directly with the restaurant by phone.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon/Thu/Fri): Lunch 11:30–14:30, Dinner 17:00–21:00. Weekends & public holidays: 11:30–20:00. Closed every Tuesday and Wednesday, plus December 31–January 1.
seats_text: Seating is entirely in private sukiya-style tatami rooms of varying sizes around a courtyard pond, roughly 60 seats total; reservations are course-only.
vegan_detail: No vegetable-only or vegan course was found in any source. The restaurant's identity and signature dish (Raijin-age) are built around shrimp and shellfish, and the standard tentsuyu dipping sauce relies on bonito (katsuo) dashi, which is not vegan. No plant-based menu or hidden-dashi accommodation is documented, so this should be treated as unsuitable for vegan diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.

bio.origin: Grew up locally in Asakusa
bio.background: Chiba began an apprenticeship as an itamae (traditional Japanese chef) at age 18 and trained for five years. At 24 he struck out on his own, opening a dining bar, and went on to run other food-and-drink businesses. In August of a recent year he founded a company called Asakusa-cho ("Asakusa Town"), deliberately registering it on the 8th of the month for its association with sue-hirogari, a fan-shaped character read as a symbol of ever-widening prosperity. Asakusa Udon, opened near the Hanayashiki amusement park in October 2025, is the company's flagship shop.
bio.specialty: Kake udon and bukkake udon (served hot, either in broth or dipping-style), to which diners add freshly fried tempura (such as kara-age chicken or shrimp) and toppings like a seasoned egg yolk or beef and shiitake mushroom.
bio.philosophy: "The town raises the people, and the people raise the town" (街が人を育み、人が街を育む) — the stated motto behind Chiba's company and this shop.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a 43-square-meter space near Asakusa Hanayashiki, with 10 standing counter spots on the ground floor and 28 table seats upstairs; it opens at 7am to serve breakfast to guests of nearby hotels and stays open into the evening for casual after-work drinking.
Chiba named his holding company Asakusa-cho and incorporated it on August 8th specifically for the auspicious, ever-widening-fortune connotation of the number 8 (sue-hirogari) in Japanese culture.

caveats: Opened October 2025, so it is very new with a thin review history. It is a traditional wheat-udon shop, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment — no dedicated gluten-free noodle, dedicated fryer, or confirmed vegan broth has been documented. Precise GPS coordinates could not be verified because the GSI geocoding service was unreachable in this session; proximity to Sensoji is based on the address and a sourced "4-minute walk" description rather than a computed distance.
closed_days: No fixed closed day found in the sources reviewed (unconfirmed — the shop appears to operate daily from 7:00-21:00, serving breakfast to nearby hotel guests through to evening drinks)
cuisine: Udon (wheat noodles) - Kanto/Kansai blend style broth
cultural_comfort_note: Sits in the heavily tourist-trafficked strip near Sensoji and Hanayashiki and markets breakfast service to nearby hotel guests, but no English menu or multilingual staff have been confirmed in available sources.
geocode_note: The GSI address geocoding endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and other geocoding services were unreachable from this session (network/proxy blocked all attempts), so coordinates could not be programmatically verified. Multiple independent sources place the shop at 東京都台東区浅草2-29-10, near Asakusa Hanayashiki and described as a 4-5 minute walk from both Sensoji and Asakusa Station — an address that sits well inside the Sensoji 0.8km target radius, but lat/lng are left null rather than estimated.
gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-flour udon shop — the noodles are made from wheat, so there is no confirmed gluten-free noodle option. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen-free process has been documented; tempura toppings appear to be fried to order in a shared fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, on-site confirmation, and even then the base noodle itself remains a wheat product.
hours_raw: 7:00-21:00 (reported at opening, October 2025)
seats_text: Roughly 38 seats total: 10 standing counter spots on the ground floor and 28 table seats on the second floor.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, broth, or preparation has been documented. Traditional Japanese udon tsuyu is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and no source confirms an exception at this shop, so vegan diners should ask staff directly about the broth base before ordering.

roles: owner/soba craftsman: Hiranuma Takayuki (name spelling per secondary sources; not independently confirmed via primary site)
bio.background: Opened in 1989 as "Asakusa Issa-an," the shop was renamed "Komagata Sobashonin" in October 1997. The owner, Hiranuma Takayuki, trained under Katakura Yasuo, a noted postwar soba craftsman credited with establishing hand-cut, thin-sliced specialty soba as a standard of the trade. The shop also runs its own soba- and udon-making school, whose 800-plus graduates have gone on to open their own shops.
bio.specialty: Five-color soba (五色そば) — a tasting set of five hand-cut noodle varieties (plain/seiro, rustic/inaka, poppy-seed/keshi-kiri, lemon/lemon-kiri, and tea/cha-kiri) — and duck nanban soba (鴨南そば) served in a yuzu-scented duck broth.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows three principles for good soba: freshly milled flour, freshly made noodles, freshly boiled water. Buckwheat is sourced from high-elevation (700m+) fields in the Aizu region of Fukushima Prefecture and stone-milled in-house on five large mills, ground slowly to preserve aroma and flavor.

anecdotes.text: About ten rotating varieties of hand-cut soba are made daily, including seiro, inaka (rustic), lemon-kiri, keshi-kiri (poppy seed), shiso-kiri, and goma-kiri (sesame).
The duck broth in the kamo-nanban soba carries a yuzu fragrance, balancing the sweetness of long green onion and duck against a carefully judged kaeshi-and-dashi blend.
Buckwheat flour comes from Aizu, Fukushima, from fields above 700m elevation, and is stone-ground in-house.

caveats: Geocoding could not be verified: the GSI address-search API and other geocoding services (Nominatim, geocoding.jp, Photon) were all unreachable in this environment due to network egress policy, so lat/lng are null despite a confirmed street address. Multiple independent sources place the shop about 50m west of Komagata Bridge and roughly 320m (5-min walk) from Asakusa Station, which is consistent with being well within the Sensoji/Asakusa target radius, but this is not a geocoded confirmation. Whether the soba is 100%-buckwheat (juwari, naturally gluten-free noodle) versus a standard wheat-bound (nihachi) blend is not confirmed by any source found — sources only confirm the buckwheat itself is 100% domestic (Aizu-sourced), which is a sourcing claim, not a flour-ratio claim. Business hours end times vary slightly between sources (14:00 vs 14:30 lunch close; 20:00 vs 20:30 dinner close). The owner's name romanization ("Hiranuma Takayuki") is corroborated by two secondary sources but not by a primary/official source, since the restaurant has no confirmed official website. Could not directly fetch Tabelog or most restaurant-info sites (403 errors on all WebFetch attempts in this environment); all facts are drawn from WebSearch-surfaced secondary sources (blogs, portal aggregators) rather than primary pages read directly.
closed_days: Mondays; also closed the 2nd and 4th Sunday of each month
cuisine: Soba — handmade soba specialist (five-color soba, duck nanban soba)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood soba craftsman's shop; no English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found in any source, so a few basic Japanese food words will help when ordering.
gf_detail: Sources confirm the buckwheat itself is sourced 100% domestically, from high-elevation Aizu (Fukushima) fields, and is stone-milled in-house, but none specify the buckwheat-to-wheat ratio of the actual noodles (i.e., whether they are juwari/100%-buckwheat or a standard nihachi blend that uses wheat as a binder). The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is the traditional kaeshi-and-bonito-dashi type; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or dedicated fryer was mentioned in any source. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the flour blend and sauce ingredients before ordering — this shop does not market itself as gluten-free.
hours_raw: Lunch approx. 11:30-14:00/14:30, Dinner approx. 17:00-20:00/20:30 (exact close times vary slightly between sources); closed Mondays and the 2nd & 4th Sunday of each month.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and alternate geocoders (Nominatim, geocoding.jp, Photon) all returned connection/policy-denial errors (blocked egress) in this environment, so coordinates could not be computed from the confirmed address (東京都台東区駒形2-7-3). Multiple independent sources describe the location as ~50m west of Komagata Bridge along Edo-dori and ~320m (5-minute walk) from Asakusa Station, which is consistent with being well inside the Sensoji-area target radius, but this has not been geocode-verified.
neighborhood: Asakusa / Komagata, near Komagata Bridge along the Sumida River

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba is served with a traditional kaeshi (soy-sauce-based tare) and bonito dashi; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative was mentioned in any available source.

seats_text: Table seating for groups of 4 and 8 near the entrance, plus a tatami-mat room in back that can seat about 20 for parties (exact total seat count not specified in sources).
vegan_detail: The broths are built on traditional katsuobushi (bonito) dashi and kaeshi, and signature dishes include duck (鴨南そば). No vegetarian or vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or meat-free tsuyu option was mentioned in any available source — standard dishes here are not vegan-safe.

bio.origin: Founded in Shōwa 11 (1936) as a drinking establishment in Asakusa's entertainment district; later became a family-run yōshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant, now in its third generation.
bio.background: ぱいち has been run by the same family for three generations since 1936, tucked in a backstreet corner one block north of Kaminarimon-dōri. It began life as a small bar rather than a restaurant.
bio.philosophy: Even as the storefront signage and neighborhood around it have changed over the decades, the family has kept the flavor and spirit of the original shop largely unchanged, serving affordable, nostalgic Shōwa-era Western food to neighborhood regulars.
bio.specialty: Beef stew (ビーフシチュー) served bubbling in an iron pot: the demi-glace sauce is specially researched and simmered for about 4 days, and the beef itself is slow-simmered for 7-8 hours for a rich but light, soup-like sauce. Also known for tongue stew (タンシチュー), menchi-katsu, and katsu sando.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name is said to come from 一杯 (ippai, "one drink") — a nod to its origins as a bar — with the word playfully reversed to create ぱいち (Paichi).
The sign advertising the restaurant's "famous beef stew" was hand-written by the current (third-generation) owner's uncle.
The restaurant has long been popular with entertainment-industry regulars connected to Asakusa's theater and performance scene, drawn in by the beef stew.

caveats: Traditional Shōwa-era yōshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation found in any source; wheat-based roux and breaded/fried dishes are central to the menu. Coordinates could not be verified via the GSI geocoder due to a network/tooling outage in this session (all WebFetch calls, including to unrelated test domains, returned 403), so lat/lng are left null despite strong textual evidence the address sits well within the Sensō-ji target radius.
closed_days: Sundays (and occasional irregular holidays)
cuisine: 洋食 (Yōshoku / Japanese Western-style cuisine)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school neighborhood eatery with an all-Japanese menu and no evidence of English signage, English menu, or foreign-language support — comfortable for Japanese speakers but not set up for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and every other WebFetch target, including unrelated control URLs like example.com, returned HTTP 403 throughout this session — a session-level network/egress issue, not something specific to this address. Coordinates could not be verified programmatically, so lat/lng are left null rather than estimated from memory. The address (Asakusa 1-chōme, one block north of Kaminarimon-dōri, near Asakusa Station on the Ginza Line) is, per multiple independent sources, solidly within Asakusa's core tourist district and well inside the Sensō-ji 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-heavy traditional yōshoku menu: the signature beef stew is built on a roux-thickened demi-glace sauce simmered for days, and other specialties (menchi-katsu, tonkatsu-style cutlets, ebi-fry, katsu sando) are all breaded in wheat panko and deep-fried. No source mentions a gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce option. Should be treated as unsafe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00 (L.O.), 17:00–20:00 (L.O.); closed Sundays
seats_text: A handful of two-top tables (around four), one larger table seating roughly six, plus counter seating along one wall.
vegan_detail: Every dish identified across sources is meat- or dairy-centered — beef stew, tongue stew, menchi-katsu, tonkatsu, gratin — and no plant-based dish, dashi substitution, or vegetarian option was mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

lead: 高級伊奈利寿司専門店 福寿家

roles: Founded by Mine Yasu (峰ヤス), 1st generation, 1922
Currently run by the 4th-generation owner (name not publicly documented)
bio.background: Founded in Taishō 11 (1922) in Asakusa by founder Mine Yasu, who coined the shop's own term "伊奈利寿司" (Inari-zushi, written with a distinctive kanji) for its inside-out style of inari sushi. The shop continued through a 2nd and 3rd generation after the war, but closed in 2009 when the aging owners could no longer continue. After about a decade dormant, the 4th generation revived it in 2020 through a crowdfunding campaign aimed at keeping the 100-year-old business alive, reopening in Hanakawado, Asakusa with a refreshed look while keeping the original recipe and technique.
bio.origin: Asakusa (Hanakawado), Tokyo
bio.specialty: Inari sushi ("伊奈利寿司") made by turning the aburaage tofu pouch inside-out to wrap vinegared rice, plus original varieties such as 塩伊奈利 (salt-seasoned inari, using a secret salt seasoning instead of the traditional simmered broth) and sweet-potato, lotus-root, and pickled-ginger inari.
bio.philosophy: The founder's technique of turning the fried-tofu pouch inside-out before wrapping it around the rice is meant to evoke "turning things around" (裏を返す), a play on words expressing the wish that customers will turn around and come back again.

anecdotes.text: The shop closed for roughly a decade starting in 2009 because the aging 2nd/3rd-generation owners could no longer continue it, and was brought back in 2020 through a crowdfunding project by the 4th generation determined to preserve the shop's 100-year history.

A visiting food blogger who expected a plain, old-fashioned inari sushi was surprised to find lotus-root and pickled-ginger varieties unlike anything they had tried, calling it proof of "the strength of a specialty shop."
The aburaage is sourced directly from specialty tofu producers in Niigata and Saitama, and the rice from Miyagi, then simmered in a housemade broth to give the pouches a juicy, umami-rich flavor.

caveats: Vegan and gluten-free specifics (exact soy sauce type, whether the simmering broth contains bonito/fish dashi) are not documented by the shop itself in any source found; celiac or strict-vegan guests should ask staff directly before ordering. Coordinates could not be verified via an automated geocoder because the network policy blocked the GSI address-search API during this session.
closed_days: Monday
cuisine: Inari sushi (specialty shop)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering, phone reservations, and available signage/menu information found online are all in Japanese; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found in any source.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session — the outbound request was blocked with HTTP 403 by network egress policy — so coordinates could not be independently geocoded. The address (Hanakawado 2-chome, Taito-ku) is generally known to be within the Asakusa district a short walk from Sensō-ji, but this specific location was not confirmed against the target-radius coordinates.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented anywhere for this shop. The aburaage tofu pouches are simmered in a housemade broth, and traditional inari sushi of this kind is typically made with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce; this has not been confirmed one way or the other for Fukujuya specifically, so it should not be assumed gluten-free.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00, or until sold out; closed Mondays
name_ja: 高級伊奈利寿司専門店 福寿家

positives.text: Every publicly described variety of the shop's inari sushi (classic broth-simmered, salt-seasoned, sweet potato, lotus root, pickled ginger) is built from tofu, rice, and vegetables, with no meat or seafood toppings mentioned in any source reviewed.

seats_text: Operates primarily as a takeout specialty shop; some secondary listings mention a small eat-in space (around two seats), but this is not consistently confirmed across sources.
vegan_detail: The core ingredients — aburaage tofu pouches, vinegared rice, and vegetable fillings such as sweet potato, lotus root, and pickled ginger — are plant-based, and no meat or seafood toppings appear in any menu description found. However, the housemade broth used to simmer the tofu pouches is not documented as dashi-free; if it uses bonito (katsuo) dashi, as is traditional for many inari sushi shops, it would not be strictly vegan. This has not been confirmed by the shop itself, so vegan diners should ask before ordering.

lead: かき氷処 浅草氷室堂 浅草本店
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-24-8 クレスト浅草 1F

bio.background: Asakusa Himurodo's Asakusa main branch opened in May 2023 in the Crest Asakusa building, a short walk from Asakusa Station. It is a dedicated kakigori (shaved ice) shop built around ice quality: the shop uses carefully selected, high-purity ice that is prized for melting almost instantly, giving the dessert what fans describe as a roughly "three-minute" window of peak texture before it's meant to be eaten.
bio.specialty: Shaved ice topped with handmade syrups made without artificial colors, flavors, or sweeteners, built around seasonal fruit. Standouts include the fresh-strawberry kakigori and an Asakusa-location-exclusive ripe sweet potato (mitsuimo) kakigori, plus a version topped with real mitarashi dango.
bio.philosophy: A focus on the purity of the ice itself and on natural, additive-free syrups made in-house, with menus that shift with the seasons to showcase fruit at its best.

anecdotes.text: The shop's ice is so fine and high-purity that it is said to have only about a three-minute window before it loses its ideal texture, a point of pride highlighted in visitor write-ups.
The Asakusa main store has a location-exclusive menu item, a fully ripened sweet potato (mitsuimo) shaved ice, alongside standards like fresh strawberry and a mitarashi-dango-topped version.

caveats: Payment method is unconfirmed: one visitor report describes cash-only, while Yahoo!Map lists card payment as accepted — verify at the door. No independent official website was found (only an Instagram account, @asakusa_himurodo, and third-party listing/reservation sites). lead: 浅草もんじゃ かのや
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-4-11（ホッピー通り）

bio.background: Run directly by a family whose roots trace back to a 150-year-old Tsukiji fish wholesaler, sourcing seafood fresh daily from the Tsukiji and Toyosu markets. The restaurant sits on Asakusa's Hoppy Street (also called Niko-dori), the retro shitamachi (old downtown) alley of cheap izakaya and monjayaki joints just west of Sensoji.
bio.origin: Asakusa / Tsukiji, Tokyo
bio.philosophy: Bring fresh, market-sourced seafood into the classic Tokyo monjayaki format, cooked tableside, in the informal, boisterous hospitality style typical of Hoppy Street's shitamachi drinking alley.
bio.specialty: 明太もちもんじゃ (mentaiko-mochi monjayaki) — spicy cod roe and mochi folded into the classic Tokyo-style monja batter
roles: Restaurant run by a family of Tsukiji seafood wholesalers

anecdotes.text: The shop is run directly by a 150-year Tsukiji fishmonger business, sourcing all seafood fresh daily from Tsukiji and Toyosu; the menu features about 10 types of monjayaki plus over 40 optional toppings, and staff prepare a fluffy tableside egg appetizer before cooking the monja.

caveats: This is a classic Tokyo-style monjayaki/seafood izakaya, not a dietary-specialty restaurant. The monja batter is wheat-flour based and cooked with dashi and soy-based seasonings on a shared teppan grill, and the menu centers on fresh seafood (the signature dish uses mentaiko fish roe). No gluten-free or vegan accommodation, alternative flour, or allergy menu was found in any source consulted, so it is unsuitable for celiac guests and not vegan-friendly as ordinarily served. lead: 浅草 江戸前 三松寿司

bio.background: A small Edomae sushi counter near Sensōji that has operated continuously since 1958 (Shōwa 33). Multiple independent write-ups describe it today as carried on by a mother-and-daughter team, with a female sushi chef at the counter — unusual in a trade historically dominated by men — which several visitors note makes the small room feel easy for solo women diners.
bio.specialty: Hikarimono (silver-skinned fish such as kohada and saba) and anago (simmered conger eel), the shop's two signature items

anecdotes.text: Founded in Shōwa 33 (1958), the shop is described as an established name now kept going by a mother-and-daughter team.
Its two 'flagship' items are hikarimono and anago, praised for soft, melt-in-the-mouth texture.
A blog post highlights the restaurant specifically for having a female sushi chef making the nigiri.

caveats: Site verification relied on search-engine snippets only: the official site, Tabelog, and blog pages all returned HTTP 403 through this session's network policy (blanket outbound block observed even on unrelated test domains like en.wikipedia.org), so no page could be fetched and read directly. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists in any source found; the assessment below is inferred from the restaurant being a traditional Edomae sushi counter, not from any statement by the restaurant itself.

Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: ドーター ブティック トーキョー
address_ja: 東京都台東区松が谷3-16-8 並木ビル1F

roles: representative director, Daughter Planning Co., Ltd.
bio.origin: Started by Horikawa after she developed her own skin sensitivity linked to certain foods and began baking gentler sweets for herself before turning it into a business.
bio.background: DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE was founded in 2014 as a vegan sweets brand, initially building a following through pop-up events, department-store fairs, and farmers markets before opening its first standalone shop, DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE TOKYO, in Matsugaya, Taito-ku in August 2018, where café-style items were added alongside its baked-goods lineup.
bio.specialty: An extensive lineup (roughly 60 varieties) of vegan, gluten-free cookies — including savory versions with vegan cheese or aonori — plus muffins, tarts, brownies, pound cakes, and gift cookie tins such as a yakuzen (herbal) cookie tin and a vegan butter cookie tin.
bio.philosophy: Sweets that are kind to the heart and body, but still 'delicious and fun' — made without wheat, dairy, or egg, using rice flour and other gluten-free flours, sweetened with beet sugar and maple syrup rather than refined white sugar, and without chemical synthetic additives.

anecdotes.text: Founder Kumiko Horikawa developed her recipes after struggling with skin sensitivity linked to certain foods, turning her personal search for gentler sweets into the DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE brand, founded in 2014.

After years of selling through pop-up events, department-store fairs, and farmers markets, DAUGHTER BOUTIQUE TOKYO opened its first standalone shop in Matsugaya, Taito-ku in August 2018, adding café-style items to its baked-goods lineup.

caveats: This is a small-batch bakery/boutique rather than a full sit-down restaurant — it functions primarily as a retail counter for pre-made and made-to-order vegan, gluten-free baked goods (café-style items were added when the shop opened), and advance phone/email ordering is recommended for pickup of specific items or gift tins. Some recent social-media signals suggest the brand may be leaning more toward online sales, but the Matsugaya storefront, phone number, and regular hours are still listed as current and active by the official site, Tabelog, and multiple directory listings as of this verification (July 2026) — confirm opening status before a special trip. Precise GPS coordinates could not be machine-geocoded during this verification (see geocode_note); location is based on the confirmed street address in Matsugaya, Taito-ku, an easy walk south of Sensō-ji/Asakusa.
closed_days: Wednesday, Sunday
cuisine: Vegan and gluten-free baked goods (cookies, muffins, tarts, brownies, pound cakes) — specialty bakery/cafe
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering (phone or email) and packaging are Japanese-first with no confirmed English menu or staff, though the shop is referenced in several English-language gluten-free Tokyo travel guides.

No specific information was found on soy sauce or dashi in the savory cookie varieties (e.g., aonori/vegan-cheese cookies), so those with additional allergies should ask staff about individual items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: As a fully plant-based bakery, no animal-derived ingredients (dairy, egg, honey) are used anywhere in the kitchen.

positives.text: Every item is made without wheat, dairy, or egg, using natural sweeteners like beet sugar and maple syrup instead of refined white sugar or artificial sweeteners, and without chemical synthetic additives.

As an all-plant kitchen there is no meat/fish/dashi cross-contact concern, though specific ingredient lists for hidden dashi (relevant mainly to savory dishes, not applicable here) were not separately confirmed.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Edo/Tokyo
bio.background: Founded in 1801 (Kyōwa 1) by Echigoya Sukeshichi, a young man from Musashi Province (present-day northern Saitama) who came to Edo at 18, worked in service, and eventually opened a modest meal shop in Asakusa's Komagata district. The business has specialized in loach (dojō) cuisine ever since and still operates from a building in the Edo-period 'dashigeta-zukuri' merchant-house style.
bio.specialty: Dojō-nabe — whole pond loach simmered tableside in a shallow iron pot in a shōyu-based broth, the dish the restaurant is named for.
bio.philosophy: Preserving Edo-period dining customs and atmosphere alongside the food itself — low tatami seating, unchanged recipes, and a building whose upper floor famously has no street-facing windows.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's distinctive four-character spelling 'どぜう' (instead of the standard five-character 'どぢやう') dates to a 1806 fire that destroyed the original shop. Considered the five-character spelling to be bad luck, the founder had a well-known signboard calligrapher write it as four characters instead; the shop's renewed popularity led other loach restaurants across Edo to copy the spelling.

During the Edo period the shogunate discouraged eating four-legged animals, so loach became an important source of animal protein for ordinary townspeople — a role the restaurant is remembered for supporting.

The main hall keeps its Edo-era merchant-house construction, and the street-facing second floor has no windows — traditionally explained as a way to avoid appearing to look down on a passing daimyo's procession.

caveats: Network policy in this session blocked direct WebFetch/curl access to Tabelog, the official site (dozeu.com), and the GSI geocoding API (all returned 403 at the proxy level), so this record relies on WebSearch result snippets (with source URLs) rather than directly fetched pages. Coordinates come from a third-party map listing, cross-checked against the independently reported location of Toei Asakusa Line Asakusa Station Exit A1 (~120m away, consistent with the restaurant's own "1 min walk from A1" claim). Straight-line distance to the given Sensōji reference point computes to ~820m, marginally over the nominal 800m check radius — treated as within tolerance given known geography (Komagata is the neighborhood immediately south of Sensōji/Asakusa Station) and reference-point precision. No gluten-free or vegan information specific to this restaurant was found in any source; the dietary assessment below is based on the restaurant being a single-protein (loach/fish) specialty restaurant using a shōyu-based broth, not on any restaurant-specific allergy statement. Also has a Shibuya branch (道玄坂), so this Asakusa location is the honten (main store), not the only location.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — a handful of days each month, announced on the official website; not closed on a fixed weekday.
cuisine: どじょう料理 (loach/dojō specialty cuisine)
geocode_note: GSI address-search API and direct WebFetch of mapping/restaurant sites were blocked by session network policy (403 at proxy). Distance to the supplied Sensōji reference point (35.7148, 139.7967) computes to ~820m via haversine, just outside the nominal 800m radius but within plausible reference-point tolerance.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found in any available source. The signature dojō-nabe and related dishes are cooked in a shōyu-based broth; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed from wheat, so the broth and most seasoned dishes should be assumed to contain gluten. No online allergy menu exists (Japanese-language only, walk-in service), so anything beyond plain grilled items would need in-person confirmation with staff, which is not guaranteed to be in English. Not recommended for celiac diners without that direct, cautious confirmation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dojō-nabe broth is a shōyu-based warishita typical of this dish style; conventional Japanese soy sauce is wheat-brewed, so the broth is not inherently gluten-free. This is general knowledge about the dish type, not a restaurant-specific confirmed statement.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and other fish-based items prepared in a shared kitchen; no vegetarian or vegan dish was identified in any menu listing found online.

seats_text: Traditional tatami-floor seating across multiple rooms, roughly 266 seats in total; private banquet rooms available for groups up to about 20.
vegan_detail: This is a single-protein specialty restaurant built entirely around dojō (pond loach) and related fish dishes cooked in fish/animal-based broth; no vegetarian or vegan dish was found in any menu source. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.

cuisine: Oden (traditional Japanese simmered dish - fish cakes, daikon, eggs, konjac, etc. in dashi broth)
vegan_detail: This is a traditional, century-old oden restaurant, not vegan-oriented. The signature broth is built on katsuobushi (bonito flake) and kombu dashi, and the majority of oden ingredients on the menu are animal-based (fish cakes, eggs, octopus, meat). No vegan menu or plant-based dashi option was found in any source consulted.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce were mentioned anywhere. The broth is seasoned with usukuchi (light) soy sauce, which contains wheat, and common oden items like chikuwa and hanpen fish cakes typically contain wheat starch as a binder. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff of every ingredient.

closed_days: Monday (regular closing day per multiple sources; some seasonal variation reported)

roles: sixth-generation owner/chef: Funadaiku Kaito (舩大工 海斗)
bio.origin: Founded in Taishō 4 (1915) when the founder, granted permission by an established oden stall on the grounds of Hōsenji temple in Osaka, brought that Kansai-rooted oden craft to Tokyo and opened shop in Asakusa's Senzoku district.
bio.background: Otafuku has operated in the same Senzoku neighborhood for over a century. Senzoku was historically the entrance district to the Yoshiwara pleasure quarter, and the shop was a haunt of geisha, their patrons, and local craftsmen — its old layout even included a separate entrance so guests wouldn't awkwardly encounter one another. The building was renovated in 2019, blending the shop's old-Tokyo atmosphere with a more modern interior while keeping the family recipe unchanged. It is now run by the sixth generation of the founding family.
bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a light (usukuchi) soy sauce and bonito-kombu dashi — a broth the shop describes as neither classic Kantō nor Kansai style but its own mellow, easy-to-drink signature, served from a rotating lineup of roughly 30-40 oden ingredients.
bio.philosophy: The current sixth-generation owner has said he learned the importance of preserving tradition from his grandfather (the fourth generation) and the importance of taking on new challenges from his father (the fifth generation) — a balance the shop tries to hold between its century-old recipe and a renewed, modernized space.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name comes from 'otafuku,' a smiling-woman motif believed to bring good fortune to a business.

Senzoku, where the shop sits, was once the gateway to the Yoshiwara pleasure district, and Otafuku was frequented by geisha, their patrons, and neighborhood craftsmen; the entrance was historically arranged so guests wouldn't run into one another.

The sixth-generation owner, Funadaiku Kaito, learned the importance of preserving tradition from his grandfather (4th generation) and the importance of taking on new challenges from his father (5th generation).
The shop uses light soy sauce and a bonito-and-kombu dashi, giving a broth that is 'mild and drinkable,' distinct from typical Kantō-style oden.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free broth option is mentioned in any source; the shop is a traditional oden restaurant with no stated allergy accommodations.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is seasoned with usukuchi (light) soy sauce, a wheat-containing product, per the shop's own description of its dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi is made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and kombu, meaning the broth itself is not vegan and would flavor any vegetable ingredients cooked in the same pot.

bio.origin: Asakusa — occupies the basement level of Hamasei Hyōan (濱清瓢庵), an established kaiseki restaurant on Kenban-dori (the old geisha-district street) just behind Sensō-ji.
bio.background: Tofu Kawakaze is the tofu-focused offshoot of Hamasei Hyōan, a kaiseki restaurant in Asakusa's historic Kenban (geisha registry office) quarter. The restaurant makes its own tofu and yuba in house rather than buying it in, built around a small, intimate dining room with tatami private rooms — one seats just two people beside a lit aquarium of goldfish and tropical fish.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen makes tofu and yuba fresh using soybeans and spring water carried in from Chichibu, aiming for a sweeter, deeper soy flavor meant to be paired with sake rather than treated as a filler ingredient.
bio.specialty: 汲み湯葉豆腐 (kumi-yuba dōfu) — hand-scooped yuba that diners lift themselves off the surface of gently simmering soy milk, alongside chilled and stacked house tofu.

anecdotes.text: The signature dish lets diners scoop their own yuba tableside as it forms on the surface of the simmering soy milk — a hands-on presentation the restaurant is known for.
The two-person private room beside a lit fish tank, with goldfish and tropical fish swimming past, is a popular reservation-only spot for couples.

caveats: Full page content on Tabelog, the official site, Retty, and Tripadvisor could not be directly fetched (blocked/403) — facts below are drawn from WebSearch snippets of those pages plus aumo, Asakusa-navi, and Hotpepper, which are consistent with one another on core details (address, hours, closed day, signature dish). No dedicated vegan or gluten-free statement was found anywhere for this restaurant — it does not appear on any Asakusa vegan/GF restaurant guide, and its course menus include chicken and fish hot pot options, so vegan/GF suitability is inferred from general knowledge of Japanese tofu-kaiseki cuisine, not a direct restaurant claim. Seat count, exact card-payment policy, and walk-in-without-reservation policy were not confirmed in any source and are marked accordingly (cash_only defaulted to false/cards-likely-accepted as the common case for this price bracket in central Tokyo, but this is not independently verified — call ahead to confirm). Coordinates are an approximation (see geocode_note) since the government geocoding API was unreachable. Celiac/severe-allergy diners should call ahead; do not rely on this listing alone for safety-critical decisions.
closed_days: Mondays (weekday lunch is by advance reservation only, for parties of 10+)
cuisine: Tofu & yuba kaiseki-style izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local tofu-kaiseki izakaya near Asakusa's old Kenban geisha quarter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — some Japanese (or a translation app) will help, especially for asking about ingredients.

Coordinates given are approximated from the neighboring Asakusa Kenban (見番) landmark at 3-33-5 Asakusa (same block, the restaurant is described in sources as sitting on the same Kenban-dori street), obtained via a Mapion map-link reference in search results (35.71741649, 139.79669). This places it roughly 290m from Sensō-ji, well within the target radius, but treat the precision as block-level, not rooftop-level.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program or wheat-free confirmation was found for this restaurant. As a traditional Japanese tofu-kaiseki/izakaya kitchen, standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) and dashi are almost certainly used in dipping sauces, simmered dishes, and hot pot broths; tofu and yuba themselves are naturally wheat-free but any accompanying tsuyu/ponzu or seasoning is a likely cross-contact and hidden-wheat risk. There is no evidence of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free menu. Celiac diners must ask staff in detail about each dish's sauce and broth before ordering.
hours_raw: Dinner: Mon–Sat 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sun/holidays 17:00–21:30 (L.O. 20:00). Lunch: Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–14:00 (weekday lunch available only by advance reservation for groups of 10 or more). Closed Mondays.

positives.text: Tofu and yuba are made fresh in house daily from soybeans and Chichibu spring water rather than bought pre-made, which at least limits hidden mass-produced additives in the tofu itself (sauces and broths are a separate, unconfirmed question).

seats_text: Small dining room with multiple tatami-style private/semi-private rooms, including a two-person room beside an aquarium; exact seat count not published.
vegan_detail: Tofu, yuba, and simple boiled/chilled preparations could work for a vegan diner in principle, but this is not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant: course menus explicitly include chicken hot pot and other animal-protein dishes, and Japanese kaiseki/izakaya kitchens routinely use katsuo (bonito) dashi in soups, simmering liquid, and dipping sauces even for tofu dishes. This restaurant does not appear on any Asakusa vegan/vegetarian restaurant guide found in research. A vegan diner would need to specifically request dashi-free preparation and confirm dipping sauces are plant-based, with no guarantee the kitchen can fully accommodate that.

lead: 浅草もんじゃお好み焼き ぽんちゃん

roles: One of five Asakusa eateries run by the local JNA Asakusa restaurant group (alongside an izakaya, a soup-curry shop, a sweets shop, and an Italian dining spot)
bio.origin: Opened in 2019 on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street
bio.background: Ponchan opened in 2019 on Hisago-dori, the covered shotengai (shopping arcade) that runs just west of Sensoji toward the Hanayashiki amusement park. It's one of five Asakusa restaurants run by the same local operator (JNA Asakusa), sitting alongside sister shops covering izakaya, soup curry, sweets, and Italian dining -- a small home-grown group rather than a national chain.
bio.specialty: Iwanori shirasu monja (a monjayaki topped with seaweed and whitebait) is called out as the signature dish, alongside teppan-grilled sides like geso batter (grilled squid legs in butter).
bio.philosophy: The shop was set up as a relaxed, welcoming teppan spot for the neighborhood -- a place where families, kids, and groups of friends gather around the hot plate together, serving the okonomiyaki and monjayaki that have long been eaten in Asakusa's shitamachi (old downtown) while also mixing in modern arrangements like tom yum kung monja and pork-curry-cheese okonomiyaki.

anecdotes.text: A small tanuki (raccoon dog) figure marks the shop's entrance on Hisago-dori and is used as the go-to landmark in local directions -- look for the tanuki just off the arcade, close to the Hanayashiki amusement park.
The dining room is described as warm, wood-lined, and unhurried, with generously sized table seating built for groups sharing a teppan together.

caveats: Direct WebFetch of Tabelog, the official site, Hot Pepper, and the GSI geocoding API all failed with proxy-level 403s during this session (a broad connectivity block affecting many hosts at once, not a per-site issue), so this record is built entirely from WebSearch result snippets rather than directly fetched pages. Two different search snippets gave conflicting weekday opening times (12:00 vs 16:00); the more detailed, dedicated-hours snippet (weekday 16:00-23:00 / weekend & holiday 12:00-23:00) was used. Coordinates could not be obtained from the GSI geocoder (blocked) and are estimated from the confirmed address block (Asakusa 2-chome, next to the Hanayashiki amusement park on Hisago-dori) rather than a geocoding API lookup -- treat as approximate. No gluten-free or vegan-specific statements from the restaurant itself were found; the GF/vegan assessment below is inferred from the cuisine type (wheat-flour monja/okonomiyaki batter, shared teppan) rather than confirmed via a direct allergy statement.
closed_days: 不定休 (no fixed weekly closed day; irregular/ad-hoc closures -- confirm before visiting)
cuisine: Monjayaki & okonomiyaki (teppan-grilled) -- a locals' hidden gem with a relaxed, easygoing atmosphere
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood monja shop with an English Tabelog listing but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff -- basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help.
geocode_note: GSI geocoding API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable (blocked at the network/proxy level) for this session, so coordinates are an estimate placing the address block (Asakusa 2-22, on Hisago-dori next to the Hanayashiki amusement park) rather than a geocoder-verified point. This estimate falls well within 0.3km of Sensoji, inside the required target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised. Monjayaki and okonomiyaki batter is wheat-flour based by definition, and standard Japanese Worcester-style okonomi sauce and soy sauce (both typically wheat-containing) are core to the dish. Diners cook together on shared teppan grills, so cross-contact with wheat batter is essentially built into the format. Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated fryer, gluten-free batter, or allergy protocol was found in any source.
hours_raw: 月～金・祝前日 16:00～23:00／土・日・祝日 12:00～23:00（フード・ドリンクともにラストオーダー22:30）。定休日は不定休。
name_ja: 浅草もんじゃお好み焼き ぽんちゃん
neighborhood: Asakusa -- Hisago-dori shopping street (about 5 minutes on foot from Asakusa Station, near the Hanayashiki amusement park)

positives.text: The menu includes a vegetable-forward monjayaki positioned as a lighter, healthier option, though this is a marketing description rather than a confirmed vegan or allergen-free dish.

seats_text: Table seating around shared teppan grills; described as spacious, wood-toned, and group-friendly, with a banquet-style area at the back seating two tables side by side, each with its own teppan. Exact seat count not published.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or certification is advertised. Traditional monjayaki dashi typically relies on bonito/katsuo-based stock and Worcester-style sauce (which can include fish/animal ingredients), and the menu features pork- and seafood-based dishes (e.g., whitebait, squid, pork curry cheese). A vegetable-heavy monja option exists and could be a starting point, but its dashi/sauce base is not confirmed to be free of animal products -- vegan diners should ask specifically about the dashi and sauce used before ordering.

bio.chef_name: Sekiya family (5th generation: Sekiya Yoshinori / 関谷吉紀)
bio.origin: Founded in 1903 (Meiji 36) in Asakusa; the current building dates to 1948 (Showa 23), making it one of the neighborhood's oldest sushi counters.
bio.philosophy: Keeps up old-school Edomae technique — simmering, vinegar-curing, and a house nitsume (simmered soy glaze) — rather than serving everything raw, using fish sourced from close to Tokyo Bay (Koshiba, Chiba, Kashima).
bio.specialty: Chirashi sushi piled with Edomae-prepared toppings, and a nitsume glaze regulars rate among the best in the city
bio.background: A century-plus-old family sushi shop near Sensoji. Its fourth-generation owner, the late Sekiya Bunkichi, was also a published food writer — his book 'Gyomi Raisan' (魚味礼讃) is a well-regarded meditation on fish and how to prepare it, and eating at the shop is described by fans as a way to taste in person what he wrote about, aroma included.
roles: 4th-generation owner Sekiya Bunkichi authored the fish essay collection 'Gyomi Raisan'
current owner is reported as 5th generation

anecdotes.text: 紀文寿司は1903年（明治36年）創業。建物自体は昭和23年に作られたもので、浅草でもっとも古い寿司店のひとつ。
四代目店主の故・関谷文吉氏は著書『魚味礼讃』で魚や魚の調理に関する博識ぶりを披露。同店で食事をすると、味や見栄えのみならず氏が重視した『香り』も含めて追体験できる。
浅草の常連の間では、東京で一番とも言われるツメ（煮詰め）と、江戸前仕事をふんだんに使ったちらし寿司で知られる。

caveats: Traditional Edomae sushi specialist with no published gluten-free or vegan program; nitsume glaze, tsume, and dashi almost certainly rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and bonito-based dashi. Anyone with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead in Japanese and confirm case-by-case rather than assume accommodation.
closed_days: 水曜 (Wednesday)
cuisine: 寿司（江戸前）
cultural_comfort_note: A century-old neighborhood sushi counter serving mostly local regulars in Japanese, with no confirmed English menu or website, so calling ahead in Japanese (or having your hotel call) will make the visit much smoother.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or wheat-free soy sauce is documented. As a traditional Edomae shop, the sushi rice itself is typically simple (rice, vinegar, sugar, salt), but the signature nitsume glaze and any simmered (nimono) dishes are almost certainly built on regular wheat-containing soy sauce, and shared prep surfaces are the norm at an old-school sushi counter. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and ask specifically about wheat-free soy sauce and cross-contact before ordering.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable through the network proxy (repeated 403 on CONNECT) despite retries, so coordinates were instead corroborated via NAVITIME's POI listing for this address, which places the shop well within the Sensoji/Asakusa target radius.
neighborhood: 浅草（東武・都営・メトロ）
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegetable-forward option is documented; this is a fish-focused Edomae sushi specialist, and its signature nitsume glaze and simmered dishes likely use bonito-based dashi, so it is not a realistic choice for a vegan diner.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-24-3 橋口ビル1F

bio.background: The shop's head chef trained at Rentama-an (蓮玉庵), a long-established soba restaurant in Ueno, before opening this small counter-only soba-ya near Asakusa's Tsukuba Express station in January 2012.
bio.origin: Ueno soba tradition (trained at Rentama-an)
bio.specialty: Seiro (chilled dipping soba) made from slightly coarse-ground buckwheat flour, praised for its smooth texture and aroma; goma (sesame) soba is another signature order.
bio.philosophy: An elegant, almost restaurant-like take on the neighborhood soba-ya, staying open past midnight so it can double as a late-night 'shime' (last bite) stop -- unusual among Asakusa soba shops, most of which close by early evening.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, 阿娑縛 (Asaba), was given to it by a Buddhist priest from nearby Sensoji Temple.
Unlike most Asakusa soba shops, which close in the early evening, Asaba stays open until around 1am, making it a favorite stop for a bowl of soba after a night of drinking.

caveats: All facts were sourced via search-engine snippets summarizing Tabelog, Retty, Hitosara, aumo, and blog pages; direct page fetches (WebFetch/curl) were blocked in this environment by the outbound proxy's org policy (403 on tabelog.com, msearch.gsi.go.jp, and even generic hosts like google.com and wikipedia.org), so nothing here comes from a fully rendered primary page -- only from search-tool summaries citing those pages. Address/phone/hours/seat-count were corroborated across multiple independent snippets. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists for this shop anywhere in search results; the GF/vegan assessment is an inference from it being a traditional wheat-blended soba-ya, not a confirmed statement from the restaurant.
closed_days: Monday
cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, locally-oriented soba-ya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so it suits diners comfortable ordering in Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated buckwheat (juwari) noodles, or dedicated fryer were mentioned anywhere. As a traditional soba-ya it almost certainly serves standard nihachi-style noodles (buckwheat blended with wheat flour) and a wheat-based shoyu dashi tsuyu. Not confirmed safe for celiac diners -- do not rely on this listing for strict gluten-free needs without calling ahead.
geocode_note: Direct geocoding via the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by the environment's outbound proxy policy (repeated 403 on CONNECT), and all WebFetch attempts to any host also failed with 403, so no page could be fetched directly for a precise geocode. Coordinates below are an estimate triangulated from WebSearch-confirmed nearby landmark coordinates: Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station A1 exit (35.713621, 139.792823) and Asakusa ROX (35.7129, 139.7927 per Wikidata) -- the restaurant's address (浅草1-24-3, Hashiguchi Bldg 1F) is on the same block/street (Fureai-dori), ~2 minutes' walk from the station, per multiple independent source snippets. This estimate places it well within 0.8km of Sensoji (~0.38km), satisfying the target-area requirement, but it is not a verified rooftop geocode.
hours_raw: 火〜日 17:00〜24:00（月曜定休）／ Tue-Sun 17:00-00:00, closed Mondays
seats_text: Small counter-style shop, about 14 seats, no private rooms.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. Soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) at traditional shops like this is standard katsuobushi (bonito) dashi-based, which is not vegan. Treat as not vegan-friendly unless confirmed otherwise on site.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草4-38-2（郵便番号 111-0032）

bio.background: 喜美松 began life decades ago as a fugu (pufferfish) restaurant before transforming into a yakitori/motsuyaki izakaya. It has now been serving the Asakusa Kannon-ura neighborhood — the quiet backstreet area just north of Sensoji Temple — for more than 47-50 years, becoming one of the shitamachi (old downtown) district's well-known offal-grilling institutions.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (shitamachi/old-downtown neighborhood behind Sensoji Temple)
bio.specialty: Pork motsu (offal) specialties: motsuyaki skewers of parts such as kashira (head), shiro (intestine) and kobukuro, alongside a long-simmered motsu-nikomi (offal stew). The shop was historically also known for boiled pork motsu-sashi (raw offal sashimi), though at least one 2024 local report noted this raw item had been discontinued from the menu.
bio.philosophy: Freshness of offal is treated as central to the food, with pork sourced directly from a pig farm in Saitama rather than through a general wholesaler.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sources its pork offal fresh, directly from a pig farm in Saitama, and is known for popular skewers of kashira, shiro and kobukuro as well as boiled motsu-sashi.

The shop's entrance is marked by a distinctive large wooden tub (taru) at the door and a red paper lantern, a recognizable local landmark reached by heading north from the Asakusa 3-chome intersection.
In a well-known ranking of Tokyo motsuyaki shops by motsuyaki writer Hamada Nobuo, the restaurant was placed as the number-two shop on the east side ("east's number two").

A 2024 local Asakusa news piece was devoted to the day the shop's long-running motsu-sashi (raw offal sashimi) item disappeared from the menu, reflecting how closely regulars track the shop's offerings.

caveats: This is a traditional pork/beef-offal (motsuyaki) izakaya with no vegan or gluten-free focus of any kind — it is included here as a small, long-running independent local spot, not as a dietary-accommodating restaurant. Every source found is a secondary listing/blog page reached via search snippets (Tabelog's own page and several other listing sites returned 403/were unreachable to fetch directly during this verification, so details rely on consistent cross-source search summaries rather than a single primary fetch). Coordinates could not be geocoded (see geocode_note) — confirm the exact pin before publishing. Reported hours vary slightly across listings (17:00 vs 17:30 open, 22:00/22:30/23:00 close); 17:00-22:30 weekdays was the most consistently repeated figure and was used here. Seating capacity and payment methods (cash vs card) were not reliably confirmed and are left blank/default rather than guessed.
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日 (Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays)
cuisine: 牛料理・もつ焼き — beef dishes and motsuyaki (grilled pork/beef offal) izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A deep-local, Japanese-language shitamachi izakaya with no confirmed English menu, English signage, or official website — visitors without at least basic Japanese or a translation app should expect a fairly unpolished, locals-first experience.
geocode_note: The required GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and all other direct page fetches (WebFetch/curl) were blocked by the network/egress proxy throughout this verification (every outbound request, including to unrelated domains like google.com, returned 403), so no coordinates could be obtained from the mandated geocoding source or any other direct fetch. lat/lng are left null rather than estimated. The address (東京都台東区浅草4-38-2) is corroborated by multiple independent listings (Hot Pepper, NAVITIME, Rakuten Gnavi) and is described in local guides as being in the 観音裏 (Kannon-ura) backstreets north of Sensoji, consistent with the target area, but this could not be confirmed programmatically.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, substitutions, or dedicated preparation were found in any available source. This is a traditional motsuyaki/yakitori izakaya whose grilling tare sauce and simmered motsu-nikomi stew would, per standard practice for this cuisine, typically rely on soy sauce (wheat); no menu or staff statement addressing this was found. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
hours_raw: 月〜金 17:00〜22:30／土・日・祝 休み (reported with minor variation across listings as 17:00-22:30, 17:30-22:00, or 17:30-23:00; 17:00-22:30 weekdays was the most consistently repeated figure)
neighborhood: 浅草 観音裏 (Asakusa, Kannon-ura — the backstreets just north of Sensoji Temple)
seats_text: Seating capacity not reliably confirmed from available sources; described generically as a small counter-and-table neighborhood izakaya.
vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu items were found in any source. The entire menu centers on pork and beef offal, yakitori-style skewers, and meat sashimi/tataki — there is no indication of any plant-based dish, substitution, or accommodation.

bio.origin: Born and raised in Asakusa, Tokyo (b. 1974)
bio.background: After training in Japan and then at Michelin-starred restaurants in France — including under chef Régis Marcon in Paris and at a one-star house in southern France — Arai returned to Japan and spent a year working as a fish broker at the Tsukiji market to save the capital he needed. In 2000, at age 26, he opened HOMMAGE in his home neighborhood of Asakusa; the restaurant was promoted to two Michelin stars in the 2018 Tokyo guide and has held that rating since.
bio.specialty: An omakase-only tasting menu that fuses classical French technique with Japanese ingredients and umami-building elements — Arai is known for working fermented soy products such as soy-sauce moromi and miso into French-style dishes rather than a single fixed signature plate.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name, French for "tribute" or "respect," reflects Arai's guiding idea: gratitude toward nature's ingredients, the producers who grow them, his mentors, his staff, his family, and the guests who return to the restaurant. He emphasizes personal relationships with producers over simply sourcing by region or prestige.

anecdotes.text: After his apprenticeship in France under chef Régis Marcon, Arai came home and worked a year as a Tsukiji fish broker purely to fund opening his own restaurant in the Asakusa neighborhood where he grew up.

Arai seasons French dishes with Japanese soy-sauce moromi and kuromame (black bean) miso from Marushin Honke, saying the fermented depth changes the whole complexity of a plate and lets him speak to both Japanese and international palates.

caveats: Coordinates could not be independently geocoded: the Japan GSI address-search API and other geocoding services (geocoding.jp, Nominatim) were all unreachable in this research session (outbound fetch tool returned 403 for essentially every host tried, including neutral test sites), so lat/lng are left null rather than estimated. The address (東京都台東区浅草4-10-5, postal 111-0032) is confirmed by multiple independent sources and by Tabelog's own listing of the area as "浅草（つくばエクスプレス）", i.e. near the Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station and within the Asakusa/Sensō-ji district, so it is very unlikely to fall outside the target radius even though it could not be numerically verified. Exact seat count and card-payment policy were not confirmed by any source fetched; cash_only is a reasonable-but-unconfirmed default for a reservation-based fine-dining restaurant that uses online booking platforms (Ikyu, Pocket Concierge). The Monday/Tuesday closure has a conditional holiday-shift exception per the source found, so days off should be reconfirmed before a visit. Several facts (bio anecdotes, allergy-intake practice) come from WebSearch's synthesis of fetched source pages rather than a direct page-by-page WebFetch, because WebFetch was non-functional for the duration of this session.
closed_days: 月曜日・火曜日（月曜日が祝日の場合は火曜日が休業日となる場合あり）
cuisine: フレンチ（和素材を織り交ぜた創作フレンチ）
cultural_comfort_note: Reservations and the pre-visit allergy/dislike questionnaire for the omakase course are handled by phone or Japanese booking platforms; no confirmed English-language menu was found, so guests who are not comfortable communicating dietary needs in Japanese may want to book through a concierge or bilingual reservation service.
gf_detail: The tasting menus season French dishes with Japanese soy-sauce moromi and miso, which typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce substitution is documented anywhere found. What is documented is that, because the course is fully omakase, the kitchen asks guests in advance about allergies and disliked ingredients and adapts the menu accordingly — so gluten-free requests can likely be raised and discussed, but there is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free track or celiac-level cross-contamination controls, so this should be treated as ask-and-verify, not a confirmed-safe kitchen.
hours_raw: ランチ 11:30〜15:00 (L.O.12:30) ／ ディナー 18:00〜22:00 (L.O.19:30)。定休日：月曜日・火曜日（月曜日が祝日の場合は火曜日が休業日となる場合あり）。コースはおまかせのみ。
geocode_note: Unable to geocode: the GSI AddressSearch API, geocoding.jp, and Nominatim were all unreachable (outbound fetch returned 403 for every domain attempted in this session, including unrelated control sites, indicating a session-level network restriction rather than an address problem). Address is confirmed textually as 東京都台東区浅草4-10-5 (postal 111-0032), within the Asakusa/Sensō-ji district near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station per Tabelog's own area label.
neighborhood: 浅草（つくばエクスプレス浅草駅近く）

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chef Arai works Japanese fermented soy products — soy-sauce moromi and kuromame (black bean) miso — into his French dishes as a seasoning base, which typically means wheat-containing soy products are present in multiple courses of the tasting menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Because the dinner course is entirely omakase, the restaurant asks guests in advance about allergies and disliked ingredients so the multi-course menu can be tailored before each visit.

positives.text: HOMMAGE holds two stars in the Michelin Guide, recognized for delicate French cooking that folds in Japanese ingredients and umami depth rooted in the chef's Asakusa upbringing.

seats_text: Seat count not confirmed by any source found; it is a small, intimate fine-dining room based on descriptions of it as a quiet, residential-street restaurant.
vegan_detail: The omakase-only tasting menus are built around seasonal French technique with fish and meat as the central protein, and no vegan menu, plant-based tasting course, or dashi/katsuo disclosure was found in any source. The kitchen does ask about dietary dislikes before service, so a vegan guest could raise the request, but there is no evidence the restaurant regularly accommodates a full vegan course — this should be treated as ask-and-hope rather than a confirmed option.

bio.origin: Founded in Ansei 1 (1854) as a teahouse inside a corner of Baion-in, a sub-temple of Sensō-ji, at the temple's front gate in Asakusa.
bio.background: Umezono began as a small teahouse on the grounds of Baion-in (a branch temple of Sensō-ji); the shop's name comes from the many plum (ume) trees that grew in the temple garden. The first-generation owner created an original sweet — awa-zenzai — that became so popular it has kept the shop running as a dedicated Japanese sweets (kanmi) specialist for more than 170 years, still on the same approach to Sensō-ji.
bio.specialty: Awa-zenzai: despite the name ("millet zenzai"), it is made not with awa millet but with mochikibi, a plump, bright-yellow grain that is steamed, half-pounded to a sticky texture, and served with slow-simmered smooth koshi-an (strained sweet red bean paste) — the dish invented by the shop's founder.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as balancing tradition and modernity — described by the family as roughly "eight parts guarding tradition, two parts pushing forward" — keeping the original anko recipe and decades-old mochi-making and steaming equipment while still updating the menu for new generations of visitors.
roles: Family-run wagashi (Japanese sweets) shop, in business under the same name since 1854

anecdotes.text: The teahouse is mentioned in Nagai Kafū's novel 'Odoriko' (The Dancer): a passage describes trying to eat oshiruko/zenzai at Umezono but finding the shop too crowded to get in — evidence of how popular it already was in that era.

The shop's mochi-making machine in the kitchen has reportedly been in use for over 50 years, and the steamers are still sourced from the same original maker, as part of keeping the traditional preparation method for the anko and mochikibi.

caveats: Geocoding note: the official GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session (network policy blocked the request), so the coordinates given were cross-checked from third-party map/listing aggregators rather than fetched live from GSI — treat as approximate though corroborated. No official allergen/cross-contamination statement from the restaurant itself was found in any source consulted; GF/vegan assessments below are inferred from the traditional composition of each dish (not a restaurant-issued allergy guarantee), so celiac and severe-allergy diners should confirm in person. Several official-site pages (asakusa-umezono.co.jp) and some blog/aggregator pages returned 403/blocked in this session and could not be fetched directly; facts here rely on search-result snippets and the pages that were reachable.
closed_days: Irregular: closed roughly twice a month on Wednesdays (月2回水曜日不定休), plus other irregular holidays; not a fixed weekly closure.
cuisine: Traditional Japanese sweets / kanmidokoro (awa-zenzai, anmitsu, mochi sweets)
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, heavily touristed Sensō-ji-front shop covered extensively by English-language travel guides (MATCHA, LIVE JAPAN, Tripadvisor), so English signage/picture menus are likely, but no confirmation of fluent English-speaking staff was found — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) could not be reached in this session (proxy/network policy returned 403 on the CONNECT). Coordinates (35.712384, 139.79628) were instead corroborated from third-party mapping/listing aggregators found via web search rather than fetched live from the government geocoder.
gf_detail: The signature dish, awa-zenzai (steamed mochikibi grain + koshi-an red bean paste), and several other menu items — warabimochi, shiratama dumplings (rice flour), and plain anmitsu/kanten-based sweets — are made from ingredients that are traditionally wheat-free (rice, azuki beans, agar/kanten, sugar). However, the same kitchen also prepares clearly wheat-containing items on the same menu (dorayaki, cha-soba/tea-infused wheat noodles), so shared-kitchen cross-contact is possible. No official gluten-free labeling, dedicated-fryer statement, or allergen chart from the restaurant was found in this research — celiac diners should ask staff directly about specific items and preparation before ordering.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Weekends/holidays: 10:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Closed approximately 2 Wednesdays per month plus irregular additional holidays.
neighborhood: 浅草1丁目 / 浅草寺門前 (Asakusa, in front of Sensō-ji)
seats_text: 82 seats, no counter seating, no private rooms
vegan_detail: The most traditional items — awa-zenzai (mochikibi + koshi-an), plain or matcha anmitsu without cream, and warabimochi with kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) — are made from ingredients that are conventionally plant-based (azuki bean paste, agar/kanten, rice-based mochi, sugar syrup), with no dashi/bonito expected in sweets preparation. However, several popular menu items (cream anmitsu, parfaits, soft-serve items like "dora soft") explicitly include dairy (ice cream/cream), and no restaurant-issued vegan menu or ingredient statement was found to confirm anko or other components are free of any animal-derived additives. Vegan diners should ask staff which specific items are dairy-free before ordering.

lead: お好み焼き 鉄板焼 つる次郎

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — a short walk from Kaminarimon Gate/Sensoji
