bio.background: An independently run okonomiyaki, monjayaki and teppanyaki restaurant in the heart of Asakusa, about three minutes on foot from Kaminarimon Gate. The main store has an open teppan counter where guests watch the cook griddle the batter directly in front of them, plus table and semi-private seating on the first floor and a tatami sunken-kotatsu banquet room upstairs. The restaurant was featured on the TV program 'Arita Tetsuhei to Kosurarenai Machi.'
bio.specialty: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki made with a chicken-and-vegetable dashi simmered for three days, used as the base of the signature batter
bio.philosophy: Built around a few carefully sourced ingredients: a whole-chicken-and-vegetable broth extracted over three days, eggs from Aichi Prefecture, fragrant domestic wheat flour, and cabbage that is hand-cut fresh daily with the cut style changed by season.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's three-day chicken-and-vegetable dashi, Aichi eggs, domestic wheat flour and daily hand-cut, seasonally-adjusted cabbage are cited as the core of its okonomiyaki and monjayaki.
The shop was covered on the TV program 'Arita Tetsuhei to Kosurarenai Machi' as an Asakusa okonomiyaki spot.

caveats: Network access to fetch the restaurant's own website pages, Tabelog, and the GSI geocoding API directly was blocked in this session (all outbound WebFetch/curl requests returned 403 policy-denial errors, confirmed via the agent-proxy status log). All facts below were reconstructed from WebSearch result snippets that quoted or summarized those pages, so exact wording/formatting from the primary source pages could not be independently double-checked, and precise lat/lng coordinates could not be obtained.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; occasional irregular (unscheduled) closures possible — call ahead to confirm
cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き・鉄板焼き (Monjayaki / Okonomiyaki / Teppanyaki)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains its own English-language official website with English blog posts, reflecting the heavy foreign-tourist traffic around Kaminarimon/Sensoji, though on-site staff English fluency was not independently confirmed.
gf_detail: The restaurant's signature okonomiyaki/monjayaki batter is explicitly built on domestic wheat flour ("国産の香り高い小麦粉") as one of its four core showcased ingredients, and no gluten-free batter alternative, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe protocol was mentioned in any source found. Wheat is a structural ingredient of the dish itself, not just an optional add-on, so this is not a safe venue for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session — outbound requests were rejected with a 403 policy-denial at the network egress proxy on every attempt. Coordinates could not be verified programmatically; lat/lng are left null. However, the address (Taito-ku, Asakusa 1-20-8) is repeatedly described across independent sources as "about a 3-minute walk from Kaminarimon Gate," placing it well within Sensoji's immediate vicinity and inside the target 0.8km radius by strong circumstantial confirmation.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sun/Holidays 11:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00). No fixed closed day, but irregular closures possible.
name_ja: お好み焼 鉄板焼 つる次郎（浅草 つる次郎 本店）
neighborhood: Asakusa (浅草), roughly 3 minutes on foot from Kaminarimon Gate / Sensoji and near Asakusa Station

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant's showcased dashi is made from whole chicken simmered with vegetables over three days, and this broth underlies the okonomiyaki/monjayaki batter — meaning the core dish is not vegan by default and cannot simply be made vegan by omitting toppings.

seats_text: 63 seats across two floors: 1F has 8 counter seats facing the teppan, three 4-person table seats (no need to remove shoes), and two 4-person semi-private seats; 2F is an all-sunken-kotatsu tatami room usable for banquets of up to 32.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented: the signature batter itself is built on a three-day whole-chicken-and-vegetable dashi (per the restaurant's own description of its core ingredients), so even a vegetable-topping order would still contain chicken-derived broth. No vegan menu, substitution, or dashi alternative was found in any source.

bio.origin: Opened September 5, 2022 in Nishi-Asakusa; run by a small Asakusa-based restaurant group that operates several concept restaurants in the neighborhood out of a renovated, more than 50-year-old private house.
bio.background: Dochaku is a three-story renovated old townhouse turned natural-wine bistro: a standing bar on the ground floor, counter and table seating on the second floor, and a private terrace room on the third floor above a small attached wine shop.
bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward Italian-French small plates built around seasonal organic produce — dishes cited by local guides include chicken liver pâté, duck/chicken gizzard confit, a chunky organic vegetable gratin, steamed organic vegetables, and lamb skewers, alongside pasta and pizza, paired with a rotating list of over 100 natural wines kept in a third-floor cellar.
bio.philosophy: The name "Dochaku" (土着) is an architectural term meaning something rooted to its particular place. The owners' interest in organic vegetables led them to natural wine as what they felt was the truest pairing for vegetable-centered cooking, and the restaurant is framed as a project meant to grow slowly with 'this place, this time, these people.' The parent group describes its broader approach as 'standing close to people and the town,' using its handful of Asakusa restaurants to build community as part of neighborhood-making.
roles: owner/operating group also runs several other concept restaurants in Asakusa

anecdotes.text: Vegetables and meat are sourced from local producers and seafood comes from the fishing port in Ofunato, with an emphasis on small-scale wine and food producers.
The restaurant's name comes from an architectural term meaning 'rooted in the land,' reflecting a concept built around organic vegetables paired with natural wine.
A blog piece frames the restaurant as a hideaway where diners feel the connection between land, season, and people ('土地と季節と人のつながり').

caveats: This is an omnivore Italian/French natural-wine bistro with an organic-vegetable emphasis — it is not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free venue, and no GF/vegan safety claims were found from the restaurant itself, so treat with normal caution rather than as a curated safe pick. Automated GSI geocoding could not be reached this session (tool/network failure), so coordinates are unconfirmed; the address and stated walking distances from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station and Ginza-line Tawaramachi Station suggest it sits in the broader Asakusa/Nishi-Asakusa area but proximity to the 0.8km Sensōji radius could not be numerically verified and should be checked before relying on it.
closed_days: Thursdays (open as usual on public holidays)
cuisine: フレンチ・イタリアン・ワインバー（オーガニック野菜、ナチュラルワイン）
cultural_comfort_note: Booking and menu are primarily Japanese-language (reservations run through Hot Pepper, Ikyu, and R-gnavi), though the Tabelog listing has an English page for browsing before you go.
gf_detail: Core menu leans on wheat: pasta and pizza are explicitly part of the lineup alongside the organic-vegetable dishes. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or celiac-safe handling was mentioned in any source checked. Assume standard wheat exposure throughout (pasta, pizza dough, likely flour-based sauces) and do not rely on this spot for celiac-safe dining without calling ahead.
geocode_note: Could not geocode: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and all other outbound page fetches returned HTTP 403 throughout this session (a tool/proxy-level failure affecting every URL tried, not a site-specific block), so coordinates were not obtained. lat/lng left null rather than estimated.
seats_text: Three floors: 1F standing counter bar, 2F counter and table seating, 3F private terrace room said to hold roughly 10 guests. No total seat count was published in the sources checked.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on seasonal organic vegetables (raw, steamed, or roasted preparations) but the kitchen also serves chicken liver pâté, gizzard confit, lamb skewers, and seafood sourced from Ofunato, so most dishes are not vegan and use of dashi/animal stock in sauces or pasta is unconfirmed. There may be vegetable-only small plates to combine into a meal, but nothing marketed as a vegan menu or vegan course was found — ask staff and expect to negotiate a plate rather than order confidently off-menu.

bio.origin: Fukui Prefecture
bio.background: The chef-owner trained for several years at Menya Shichisai (麺や七彩), the influential Tokyo ramen shop widely credited with pioneering MSG-free (無化調) ramen broths in the 2000s, before striking out on his own. The shop first opened near Nishi-Asakusa in December 2016, earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing (Tokyo 2018) and repeated placement on Tabelog's Ramen TOKYO 100 list, then relocated a short distance to its current Tawaramachi counter in April 2023 after closing the original space in July 2022.
bio.philosophy: No chemical seasoning (無化調) — broths are built purely from whole chicken and aromatics, and the noodles are hand-kneaded fresh, one portion at a time, from a custom blend of Hokkaido strong, medium, and whole-grain wheat flours rather than bought pre-made.
bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen with medium-thick, hand-kneaded (手もみ) crimped noodles in a clear whole-chicken (marudori) broth; the shio (salt) version layers in chicken-and-seafood depth, and a thinner straight-noodle option is also offered.

anecdotes.text: The medium-thick hand-kneaded noodles are massaged individually, portion by portion, giving them a firmer, almost wonton-like chew and a pronounced wheat aroma that the shop considers central to how the noodle carries the soup.
As a nod to his native Fukui Prefecture, the owner added Fukui-style oroshi soba (grated daikon soba) and a house-made-sauce pork katsudon to the menu alongside the ramen.
The shop closed its original Nishi-Asakusa location on July 31, 2022 and reopened in its current Tawaramachi spot on April 19, 2023, just off the Tawaramachi intersection near Ginza Line Exit 1.

caveats: Tabelog ID given in the brief (13203387) is now Tabelog's own "relocated" notice page for the shop's former Nishi-Asakusa location; the shop's live, current listing (used here) is at ID 13284327 for the Tawaramachi address. Seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (roughly 7 to 10 counter seats) so it is left unspecified. Payment methods (cash-only vs. cashless) could not be confirmed from any source checked. Coordinates are a manual estimate (see geocode_note) because Japan's GSI address geocoder was unreachable from this session. This is a traditional wheat-noodle, chicken-broth ramen shop with no documented gluten-free or vegan accommodation — it is included here for its no-chemical-additive (無化調), scratch-made ingredient ethos, not for dietary accessibility.
closed_days: Sunday, Monday
cuisine: Ramen — shoyu and shio ramen with hand-kneaded wheat noodles and whole-chicken broth
cultural_comfort_note: Orders and queue position are managed through a Japanese-language touch-panel ticket system before you're called to the counter; no English menu is documented, so recognizing ramen terms (shoyu, shio, noodle thickness) or pointing at photos helps.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop. The hand-kneaded noodles are made from a blend of Hokkaido strong, medium, and whole-grain wheat flours, and the tare is soy-sauce based (standard Japanese shoyu, which typically contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated prep area, or any gluten-free accommodation is mentioned in any source reviewed — not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Japan's GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable from this session (network policy blocked the connection on every attempt). Coordinates are a manual estimate based on the shop's documented location — about 1 minute / ~130m from Tokyo Metro Ginza Line Tawaramachi Station Exit 1, address 東京都台東区寿2-9-15 — cross-checked against known Tawaramachi Station coordinates. Treat as approximate, not survey-grade.
hours_raw: 火〜金 11:00-14:30／18:00-20:00、土・祝 11:00-16:00（材料がなくなり次第早仕舞いの場合あり）、定休日：日曜・月曜

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tare is standard Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat; no wheat-free or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is documented for this shop.

positives.text: The broth is intentionally made without added MSG or chemical seasoning (無化調), relying on whole-chicken stock and aromatics for flavor — a plus for diners sensitive to MSG, though unrelated to gluten or animal-product content.

seats_text: Small counter-only seating (sources vary, reporting roughly 7 to 10 seats)
vegan_detail: The signature broths are built on whole-chicken (marudori) stock, and the shio version also carries seafood/dashi notes (鶏魚介); the menu also includes a pork-cutlet katsudon side. No plant-based broth, noodle, or vegan menu item is documented in any source reviewed — not suitable for vegan diners.

roles: owner-chef and his wife run the dining room together
bio.origin: Southern France (Provence-inspired) home cooking
bio.background: Lethe is a small couple-run bistro near Kaminarimon: the chef trained in Southern French cooking and built the menu around that region's home-style classics, while his wife looks after the floor and guests.
bio.specialty: Duck confit cured and cooked over four days, and a hearty cassoulet built on house-made sausage, alongside a bouillabaisse.
bio.philosophy: The name Lethe comes from the river of forgetfulness in Greek mythology — the owners chose it because they want guests to forget their daily worries and linger unhurried over a slow meal and good wine.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name is drawn from Lethe, the river of oblivion in Greek myth, reflecting a wish for diners to set aside daily stress and relax over dinner.
A blogger visiting for a birthday dinner singled out the cassoulet and bouillabaisse as the house specialties.
The bistro opened near Kaminarimon in spring 2022 as a hideaway serving Southern French regional dishes and wine.

caveats: Closing days are reported inconsistently across sources (some say Monday only, others Monday+Tuesday) — confirm before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information is published anywhere for this restaurant; it is a classic meat-and-wheat-heavy Southern French bistro, so treat both dietary ratings as low-confidence defaults rather than confirmed accommodations. Precise GPS coordinates could not be verified because the geocoding service was unreachable in this environment; area placement relies on distances-to-station figures published by multiple listing sites.
closed_days: 月曜・火曜定休（祝日は営業する場合あり）とする情報源が多いが、火曜営業とする情報源もあり差異あり。来店前に要確認。
cuisine: ビストロ（南仏料理）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, Japanese-run neighborhood bistro with no evidence of an English menu or multilingual staff online, so reservations and ordering are likely smoothest in Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free process is documented anywhere. As a classical Southern French bistro, dishes routinely involve wheat (bread service, roux-thickened sauces, house-made sausage and cassoulet). Diners with celiac disease or wheat allergy should call ahead and discuss ingredients directly, since no verified gluten-free accommodation exists.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and alternate geocoders were unreachable in this environment (all connection attempts were blocked at the network/proxy level), so precise coordinates could not be confirmed directly. Multiple independent listings place the restaurant at 東京都台東区雷門2-9-2, about 0.39km from Asakusa Station and 0.37km from Tawaramachi Station — an address block immediately adjacent to Kaminarimon Gate and well within 0.8km of Sensō-ji, so the area match is high-confidence despite the missing coordinates.
seats_text: Table seating for 12 plus a 5-seat counter (17 seats total)
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Southern French meat classics — duck confit, house-made pork sausage, cassoulet, and bouillabaisse — with no vegan dishes, plant-based substitutions, or dashi/bonito disclosures mentioned in any available source. Not a realistic option for vegan diners.

cuisine: Soba (hand-made buckwheat noodles) and Japanese kappo-style cuisine, with sake-pairing 'soba-mae' small dishes
neighborhood: Asakusa (Oku-Asakusa / Kannon-ura), near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, west of the Asakusa 4-chome intersection
vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-kappo restaurant built around bonito/katsuobushi-based dashi for the soba dipping sauce, and the seasonal menu centers on fish and meat (conger eel, sweetfish, breaded pork cutlet, seasonal seafood from Tsukiji). No vegan dishes, plant-based dashi, or vegan menu options were found in any source.
gf_detail: The signature noodle is hand-made 'nihachi' (2:8) soba, meaning it is roughly 20% wheat flour by design, not gluten-free. Fried items on the menu (e.g. breaded pork cutlet) appear to use wheat-based breading/batter, and soy sauce used in the tsuyu dipping sauce is standard (wheat-containing) as is typical for soba shops. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific accommodation was mentioned in any source found.
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草4-37-8 小田井ビル1F
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and other geocoding services were unreachable in this session due to network egress policy restrictions, so coordinates could not be independently verified. Based on the address (Asakusa 4-chome) and its described location near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station (approx. 35.714, 139.793, sourced from a route-planning link) just west of the Asakusa 4-chome intersection, the restaurant appears to fall well within 0.8km of Sensoji, but this is a geographic inference, not a verified geocode.
seats_text: A small counter for about 8, plus a compact tatami seating area for roughly a dozen more — an intimate, informal neighborhood soba-zake room.
hours_raw: Lunch: Sunday only, 11:30-14:00 (occasionally closed for lunch, calling ahead is recommended). Dinner: Monday, Tuesday, Thursday-Saturday, 18:00-23:00 (last order 22:30, or until the soba sells out).
closed_days: Wednesdays (also no dinner service on Sunday — Sunday is lunch-only)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, locally-loved counter soba shop with a Japanese-language-first experience and reservations customary for the evening course, though it can be booked through English-capable platforms.

bio.background: The restaurant is run by a female owner who came to soba from an unrelated career in the beauty industry. She took up soba-making almost by chance, and her natural aptitude was striking from the start — accounts describe her impressing trained soba craftsmen within just a couple of days of lessons, and completing her training in about 20 days before deciding to open her own shop in July 2015.
bio.specialty: Hand-made 'nihachi' (two parts wheat to eight parts buckwheat) soba, served alongside a seasonal 'soba-mae' selection of sake-pairing dishes made from ingredients sourced fresh each day, such as sweetfish, conger eel, and breaded pork cutlet.
bio.philosophy: Despite her unconventional path into the trade, she is described as studious and uncompromising about her craft — closely attentive to the quality of her buckwheat flour and continually refining her noodle-making technique.

anecdotes.text: After only about two days of soba lessons her noodles were already drawing praise, and she went on to impress professional soba craftsmen with difficult techniques before completing her training in roughly 20 days and opening the restaurant in July 2015.

The restaurant was featured on TV Tokyo's food-and-drink drama 'Wakako Zake,' which highlighted the proprietress hand-rolling the soba and her lively banter with the restaurant's master during service.

caveats: All external page fetches (WebFetch) were blocked by network egress policy in this session for every host tried, including Tabelog, the official site, and a GSI geocoding call; all facts here were sourced from WebSearch's synthesis of indexed page content (with source URLs) rather than from directly fetched pages, and coordinates could not be verified via the GSI API — only a geographic inference is offered.

lead: ニュープラシッダ 浅草店

bio.origin: Nepal / North India (kitchen staffed by Nepali and North Indian cooks)
bio.background: An Indian-Nepali curry restaurant tucked into a quiet residential corner of "oku-Asakusa" (inner Asakusa), a short walk from Senso-ji in the Hanakawado neighborhood. Local write-ups describe it as a long-running, regulars-favored spot (cited as roughly a decade-plus in the neighborhood) known for authentic North Indian and Nepali cooking rather than a tourist-facing curry chain.
bio.specialty: Biryani and cheese naan are called out as the standout orders, alongside freshly baked, fluffy naan served with a lineup of about a dozen curries (chicken, mutton, keema, seafood, dal, vegetable) spiced from mild to very hot across five heat levels.

anecdotes.text: "奥浅草の静かな一角にある本格インドカレー屋さん" — described as an authentic Indian curry restaurant set in a quiet corner of inner Asakusa.
Offers home-delivery and fresh-baked naan with curry bento takeout to the Asakusa/Hanakawado area.

caveats: Geocoding APIs (GSI address search, Nominatim) and direct WebFetch to the primary source pages (Tabelog, official site, hitosara, gnavi, asakusa-navi, Wikipedia test) were all blocked by network egress policy in this session (403 on every CONNECT attempt), so all facts here were assembled from search-engine snippets referencing those pages rather than full page fetches, and coordinates are a manual neighborhood-level estimate rather than an API geocode. Seat count (48) and some hours phrasing come from aggregated listing snippets that could not be independently cross-checked page-by-page. No gluten-free or vegan-specific safety claims (dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, cross-contamination protocol) were found in any source — treat gf/vegan info as directional only and confirm with staff before dining.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day (open daily); irregular closures reported around New Year's (dinner closed Dec 31, closed Jan 1–2)
cuisine: インドカレー・ネパール料理（ベジタリアン対応）
cultural_comfort_note: Run by Nepali/North Indian staff serving a largely local Japanese clientele near Senso-ji; delivery-platform listings (Uber Eats, Demae-can, Wolt) show some English dish names, but on-site English fluency and picture/allergy menus are unconfirmed, so expect basic communication rather than full English support.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free-soy-sauce information was found in any source. Curries are typically rice- or naan-based; naan itself is wheat and not an option for celiac diners, and Indian-style curries can be thickened or finished with wheat-containing ingredients depending on the recipe, so gluten status of any given curry is not confirmed. Rice-based orders (skipping naan) are plausible but unverified as gluten-free, and shared-kitchen cross-contact has not been addressed by the restaurant in any available source. Anyone with celiac disease should call ahead (03-6313-0040) and ask specifically about flour/soy sauce use and cross-contamination before ordering.
geocode_note: GSI address-search and Nominatim APIs were both blocked by network policy in this session, so the address (東京都台東区花川戸2-9-6) could not be run through an automated geocoder. Coordinates given are a manual estimate for the Hanakawado 2-chome area (between Senso-ji and Tobu Asakusa Station / Matsuya Asakusa), based on multiple independent listings describing the restaurant as a short walk from Senso-ji — this puts it well within the 0.8km Sensoji radius, but the point should be re-verified against a real geocoder before being trusted for precise mapping.
name_ja: ニュープラシッダ 浅草店
seats_text: Approximately 48 seats (about 6 at the counter, 42 at tables), per aggregated listing data — not independently confirmed on the restaurant's own site.
vegan_detail: Vegetable curry (ベジタブル) and dal (豆) are the vegetarian curry options, available both dine-in and as part of the takeout bento lineup, but no source confirms they are made without ghee, butter, cream, or other dairy/animal fat, which are common in North Indian-style curries. Naan is not vegan by default in most Japanese Indian restaurants (often contains dairy/egg). Vegan diners should ask staff directly whether the vegetable curry and dal can be prepared without ghee/butter/cream and whether rice can replace naan.

lead: 中華ソバ ビリケン
address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門2-4-1 岡安ビル1F

bio.origin: Trained at the acclaimed ramen shop Itsuki in Ōme, western Tokyo
bio.background: Kimotomoto met his wife while the two trained together at Ramen Itsuki in Ōme. After going independent as a couple, they first opened the salt-ramen shop Ramen Kai in nearby Kuramae, then in July 2019 opened this duck-broth sister shop a minute from Kaminarimon in Asakusa.
bio.specialty: Duck-and-chicken shoyu ramen with hand-kneaded noodles and duck chashu
bio.philosophy: The kitchen is known for skipping chemical seasoning (no MSG/artificial flavor boosters), building the soup's savoriness from duck and chicken stock and a housemade soy tare instead.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened July 5, 2019 as the second, sister location of the well-known Kuramae salt-ramen shop Ramen Kai, run by the same husband-and-wife team, but with its own duck-and-chicken shoyu concept.

Kimotomoto and his wife trained together at Ramen Itsuki in Ōme and married after becoming independent; local food blogs describe the shop as a labor of love built around duck stock, hand-kneaded noodles and juicy duck chashu.
A ramen blogger writing under the title 'no-chemical-seasoning ramen' described the shop as arguably Asakusa's top no-MSG ramen destination.

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation found in any source — duck/chicken stock, wheat noodles, and standard shoyu tare are all confirmed; not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diners. Direct GSI geocoding API and several primary source pages (Tabelog, Retty, ramendb, san-tatsu, VACAN, hotpepper, hamoni, gnavi) could not be fetched directly during this session due to a network/proxy restriction that blocked most outbound HTTPS fetches (including unrelated control sites like Wikipedia), so identity/logistics facts rely on WebSearch-surfaced snippets from those pages rather than direct WebFetch reads, and coordinates were cross-sourced from a menu-tokyo.jp listing rather than the GSI API.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; irregular one-off closures are announced via the shop's Instagram/X accounts, so it's worth checking social media before visiting
cuisine: Ramen (duck and chicken shoyu broth, hand-kneaded noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering and menu are primarily Japanese-language; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though it sits in a heavily touristed part of Asakusa near Kaminarimon.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session — the proxy rejected the CONNECT with a 403 policy denial on every retry (the same blanket restriction also blocked unrelated control fetches like Wikipedia during this session, so it appears to be an infrastructure/session issue rather than a GSI-specific block). As a fallback, coordinates were taken from a menu-tokyo.jp (EAT Tokyo) restaurant listing for this shop (lat=35.70907280, lng=139.79569670), which is consistent with the Kaminarimon 2-4-1 address and sits about 0.6km from Sensō-ji, within the target radius.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles (hand-kneaded, not gluten-free) and a conventional soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute, gluten-free menu, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00-15:00 & 17:30-21:00; Sat–Sun 11:00-21:00 (no fixed closed day; occasional irregular closures posted on Instagram/X)
name_ja: 中華ソバ ビリケン

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature tare is a standard soy-sauce (shoyu) base built for a duck-and-chicken broth ramen; no wheat-free or tamari-based alternative was mentioned in any source, so the soy sauce used should be assumed to contain wheat as is typical for Japanese ramen tare.

seats_text: Small counter seating (11 seats)
vegan_detail: The signature broth is built from duck and chicken stock, and the chashu topping is duck — the dish is fundamentally meat-based with no vegetarian or vegan option mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1丁目（浅草公園通り沿い、正確な番地は未確認）

bio.origin: Hiroshima Prefecture
bio.background: Miyuu, the proprietress, opened this counter-only obanzai spot on Asakusa's Kōen-dōri in May 2026. She hand-cooks a daily-changing lineup of 5-8 obanzai (home-style Japanese side dishes), leaning on seasonal vegetables, homemade miso, and ingredients sourced from her home prefecture of Hiroshima.
bio.specialty: A rotating selection of 5-8 handmade obanzai dishes, chosen by the guest from large plates set along the counter, capped off with the chef's-recommendation 'Aji-Yuu Mankitsu' course (obanzai, a signature dish, rice, dessert, and a drink).

anecdotes.text: Guests pick their dishes from large plates lined up on the counter — a self-selection obanzai format — with the 5-8 items on offer changing daily depending on what's in season.
Miyuu curates her sake list around breweries in Fukushima and Hiroshima that she came across through personal connections, pairing them with her home-style cooking.

caveats: Newly opened (May 2026), tiny 8-seat counter — reservations strongly advised. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan program was found; traditional seasonings (soy sauce, homemade miso, dashi) are likely used and have not been confirmed wheat-free or fish-free, so celiac and vegan diners should call ahead and confirm with staff before visiting.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cuisine: Izakaya / Obanzai — handmade, daily-changing seasonal vegetable dishes and homemade miso, with ingredients from Hiroshima Prefecture
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter-only izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and phone-only reservations — most comfortable with a Japanese-speaking companion or guide rather than walking in solo without any Japanese.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by this session's network egress policy (403 on every attempt, including a chōme-level retry), so it could not be queried directly. The coordinates given are an approximate chōme-level centroid for 台東区浅草1丁目 obtained via a secondary web geocoding lookup, not a precise street-address geocode from GSI.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The kitchen uses homemade miso and traditional seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi (which can contain wheat and bonito) are likely used unless the vegetable-forward dishes happen to avoid them. Because the menu changes daily and nothing about wheat-free preparation is documented, celiac diners should call ahead (050-5869-7791) and confirm which of the day's dishes are safe.
hours_raw: 16:00–23:00, closed Tuesdays
neighborhood: Asakusa, near Sensō-ji / Kaminarimon, along Kōen-dōri (Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station area)

positives.text: The daily menu emphasizes seasonal vegetables and homemade miso, giving it a naturally plant-leaning lean-up, though this has not been documented as a formal vegan or allergy-safe program.

seats_text: Counter seating only, up to 8 guests
vegan_detail: No confirmed all-vegan menu. The obanzai are vegetable-heavy and change daily, but obanzai izakaya menus typically also include fish- or meat-based items and traditional dashi (often bonito-based), and no source confirms a katsuo/dashi-free or fully plant-based option. Vegan diners should ask staff which of the day's dishes are free of fish stock, bonito flakes, and animal products.

bio.background: Founded in 1852 (Kaei 5) near Kaminarimon as a dry-goods and grain merchant trading in azuki beans and other staples; sweets made from the shop's own azuki stock gradually became the main business. In 2009 the sixth-generation owner renamed the shop from "Nishiyama Kashi-ten" (Nishiyama confectionery store) to the more approachable "Kanmidokoro Nishiyama."
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.philosophy: Carries on a tradition, dating back to the end of the Edo period, of carefully selecting quality ingredients and preparing them by hand.
bio.specialty: Tokusei Nishiyama Anmitsu (特製西山あんみつ) — coarse-strained (tsubu-an) and smooth-strained (koshi-an) housemade red bean pastes served with two flavors of housemade ice cream and mochi dumplings (shiratama), alongside seasonal zenzai and kakigori (shaved ice with matcha syrup and red bean).

anecdotes.text: The shop began in 1852 as a dried-goods and grain merchant near Kaminarimon dealing in azuki beans; sweets made from that same azuki stock gradually grew into the main business, eventually becoming today's kanmi-dokoro (sweets parlor).
In 2009 the sixth-generation owner changed the shop's name from "Nishiyama Kashi-ten" to "Kanmidokoro Nishiyama" to make it feel more approachable to customers.

caveats: Could not directly fetch the official site, Tabelog page, or GSI geocoding API in this session (outbound network/proxy failures on essentially all hosts except web search); all facts below come from web-search snippets of the official site (asakusa-nishiyama.com), shinise.tv, and co-trip.jp, cross-checked across multiple independent search results. Coordinates are a chome-level estimate (see geocode_note), not a house-number-precise geocode. Payment method (cash-only vs. card-accepted) was not confirmed anywhere; "false" is an unconfirmed default, not a verified fact. No direct statement from the shop about gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found — the GF/vegan assessment below is inferred from the traditional recipe composition (azuki, agar, rice-flour dango, brown sugar syrup) reported in secondary sources, not from the shop's own allergen disclosures.
closed_days: Wednesdays (open on Wednesdays that fall during the New Year holiday period or that coincide with Asakusa festivals/national holidays)
cuisine: 甘味処（あんみつ・ぜんざい・かき氷・自家製餡）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, Japanese-oriented neighborhood sweets shop near Sensoji with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its location on the main Kaminarimon approach means tourists visit regularly and pointing at photos/menu items should work fine.
geocode_note: The GSI Address Search API and all other direct-fetch tools were unreachable this session due to an outbound network/proxy failure (confirmed even for unrelated hosts like google.com), so a live geocode of the exact address could not be performed. Via web search, the nearest available reference point in the same block, 雷門2丁目20-4 (adjacent to the target 2-19-10), returned coordinates 35.710232, 139.797683 from a geocoding lookup site indexed in search results. This is used as a close proxy; it places the shop about 0.5km from Sensoji's main hall, consistent with its well-known real-world location directly on the Kaminarimon approach to Sensoji.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, signage, or celiac-safety statement was found from the shop itself. The signature anmitsu, zenzai, and kakigori are built from ingredients that are traditionally wheat-free — housemade azuki paste (tsubu-an/koshi-an), kanten (agar) jelly, rice-flour shiratama dumplings, and kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) — but this has not been confirmed with staff, and there is no information on shared prep surfaces, cross-contact, or whether any items (e.g., the shop's fukufuku manju buns, which are wheat-based) are prepared in the same space as the sweets. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering; this is not a gluten-free-verified kitchen.
hours_raw: 10:00〜19:00、定休日：主に水曜日（年末年始・浅草の行事や祝日と重なる水曜日は営業）
name_ja: 甘味処 西山（旧店名：西山菓子店）
neighborhood: 浅草・雷門周辺 (Asakusa, near Kaminarimon)

positives.text: Anko (red bean paste), ice cream, and other sweets are made in-house daily using carefully selected raw ingredients, per the shop's own long-stated approach to sourcing.

seats_text: Roughly 20 seats total: counter seating plus a mix of two- and four-person tables.
vegan_detail: This is not a vegan-focused shop and no vegan menu or labeling was found. The housemade red bean pastes, mochi/shiratama dumplings, agar jelly, and kuromitsu syrup are typically plant-based in traditional preparation, so a plain anko or mitsumame-style order may work with a modification request, but the anmitsu's signature housemade ice cream is dairy-based, and there is no confirmation of egg/dairy-free status for other components (e.g., any glaze or filling in the manju buns). As a sweets shop rather than a savory kitchen, hidden dashi/bonito is less of a concern than at a typical Japanese restaurant, but this has not been confirmed with staff.

cuisine: Tempura and tendon (tempura rice bowl) — Edo-style tempura specialty restaurant
neighborhood: Asakusa, Taito-ku (3-min walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, beside Hanayashiki / Hisago-dori entrance)
gf_detail: Tempura here is coated in a conventional wheat-flour batter, and the tendon is finished with a traditional simmered tare sauce that at shops of this style is almost always soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). No gluten-free batter, GF soy sauce, or dedicated fryer is mentioned anywhere in the restaurant's own materials, menus, or reviews found; nothing suggests any celiac-safe accommodation exists. Treat as not safe for celiac/strict gluten-free diners unless staff can confirm otherwise in person.
vegan_detail: The menu includes some vegetable-only tempura pieces (e.g. nanohana/rapeseed-flower tempura, seasonal kakiage vegetable fritters), but the restaurant's identity and signature dish (Godaime Tendon) are built around conger eel (anago) and other seafood, and no source confirms the tare/dipping sauce or any dashi used is free of bonito/fish stock. There is no vegan menu, vegan labeling, or vegan-specific accommodation mentioned anywhere found. Vegetable tempura items may work for a flexible/pescatarian-leaning vegetarian but should not be assumed vegan without asking staff about the sauce and any shared dashi.
hours_raw: 10:30〜21:30（料理L.O. 21:00、ドリンクL.O. 21:00）、年中無休（たまに臨時休業の場合あり）
closed_days: None listed as a fixed regular closing day — open daily (年中無休), though the restaurant notes occasional irregular/temporary closures.
seats_text: Around 40 seats total, including a small counter (about 4 seats), regular table seating, and some tatami/zashiki-style seating.
cultural_comfort_note: A well-known, tourist-trafficked tempura spot steps from Sensoji/Hanayashiki with an English-language Tabelog listing and online reservation options, so basic English/picture-menu navigation is realistic even without Japanese.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session (network egress to it was blocked), so coordinates could not be verified programmatically and are left null rather than invented. The confirmed address, 東京都台東区浅草2-15-1, is described in multiple sources as "beside Hanayashiki, at the Hisago-dori entrance," a well-known landmark cluster immediately north of Sensoji's main hall — consistent with being well inside the 0.8km Sensoji target radius, but this has not been confirmed via a geocoder.
caveats: The "秋光" name is shared by a small family of related but distinct shops/brands in Asakusa (e.g. a separate "天麩羅バル秋光" tempura-bar location, Tabelog ID 13217733, and the original "下町天丼秋光" ROX 3G shop) — this record is specifically for the Tabelog listing 13244823 / 浅草本店 (Honten) at Asakusa 2-15-1. The founding chef has also franchised the Akimitsu tendon concept to several overseas locations (Singapore, Philippines, Australia, South Korea, Canada); this Asakusa flagship remains the original, founder-run shop. Coordinates are unverified (see geocode_note) — confirm exact pin before publishing precise map placement.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Chef-owner Akimitsu Tanihara began working part-time at Dote no Iseya, a tempura shop in Asakusa's old Yoshiwara district founded in 1889 (Meiji 22), at age 18. He trained there for 17 years and was named the shop's fifth-generation master in 2004. In March 2015, at age 35, he left the shop he had led for a decade to open his own restaurant, Shitamachi Tendon Akimitsu, inside the Asakusa ROX 3G complex, carrying the same Meiji-era recipe and frying technique forward under his own name.
bio.specialty: Godaime Tendon (五代目天丼, "Fifth-Generation Tempura Bowl") — a rice bowl built around a large simmered conger eel (anago) tempura plus seasonal vegetable and seafood tempura in a housemade tare; a daily 5-serving-only 'Extreme' version is also offered.
bio.philosophy: Continuing the Meiji-era tempura tradition learned over 17 years of apprenticeship, with stated attention to the rice, seasonal ingredients, tare sauce, and frying technique.
roles: 5th-generation tempura master (trained at Dote no Iseya, now runs his own restaurant)

anecdotes.text: Tanihara started as a part-time high-schooler at the 1889-founded Dote no Iseya tempura shop, eventually training 17 years and being named its fifth-generation master in 2004.
In March 2015 he left that shop to open Shitamachi Tendon Akimitsu in Asakusa's ROX 3G building, later expanding the brand overseas.
The restaurant promotes its tempura course with a 'national donburi grand prix gold award' (全国丼グランプリ金賞) callout on its own course-menu listing.

bio.chef_name: 椿豪（つばき たけし）・椿澄子（つばき すみこ）夫妻
bio.background: Couple-run bistro opened in 2016 in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreets. Before opening, the chef worked at a restaurant in Kagurazaka, and the couple say they were inspired toward an à la carte, drop-in dining style after visiting a restaurant in Ebisu around a decade before opening their own place.
bio.specialty: Seasonal-vegetable-driven French cuisine paired with natural wine, using produce and meat from small-scale producers and seafood landed at Ofunato port.
bio.philosophy: The name しみいる (from 沁み入る, "to seep in / soak in") reflects the aim of serving food and wine that quietly seep into the heart, in a calm, unhurried hideaway atmosphere down a narrow alley behind Sensō-ji.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is counted among what food writers call the "Kannon-ura French Four Heavenly Kings" (観音裏フレンチ四天王), a small cluster of well-regarded French bistros in this quiet Asakusa backstreet.

Featured by Asakusa High School teacher Kanbayashi-sensei in dancyu's "Asakusa Lunch Guide" series as an example of authentic French cooking in the Kannon-ura area, part of a project in which he ate lunch at 708 Asakusa restaurants.
Profile piece describing the couple's cooking as food that "seeps into every organ" (五臓六腑にしみいる), built on a deliberately à la carte menu to build repeat regulars.

caveats: Hours and closed-day details vary slightly between listing sites (Tabelog, Hitosara, Hot Pepper), likely reflecting seasonal adjustments and irregular (不定休) closures on top of the fixed Monday closure — call ahead to confirm. No official site page could be directly fetched in this session (blocked by network policy), so official-site details are drawn from search-engine indexing of https://www.simiil.com/ rather than a direct read of the page. Precise GPS coordinates could not be verified because the GSI geocoding service was unreachable in this session; the address is corroborated by multiple independent restaurant-listing sites as being in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreets near Sensō-ji.
closed_days: 月曜定休、他不定休あり（Closed Mondays, plus irregular additional closures）
cuisine: フレンチ・ナチュラルワイン（季節野菜、隠れ家ビストロ）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, Japanese-language hideaway bistro down a narrow alley — English-language reservations are possible through platforms like TableCheck, but don't expect an English menu or much spoken English on site.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or dedicated gluten-free preparation was found in any source. As a classic French bistro, sauces, roux, and bread service commonly involve wheat flour, and no information on cross-contamination handling or a dedicated fryer was available. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm case-by-case; do not assume any dish is safe without asking.
neighborhood: 浅草・観音裏（Tsukuba Express 浅草駅エリア）
seats_text: Small counter-and-table bistro, about 15 seats (counter seating plus a couple of tables)
vegan_detail: The restaurant emphasizes seasonal vegetables from small-scale producers, but it is a classic French bistro built around meat and Ofunato-sourced seafood, with butter- and cream-based techniques typical of the cuisine. No vegan menu or plant-based tasting course was found in any source, so vegan diners should contact the restaurant in advance to ask what can be adapted; do not assume dishes are free of animal-based stock, butter, or dairy.
geocode_note: The GSI address geocoder (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session due to network/proxy policy, so precise coordinates could not be independently verified — lat/lng are left null rather than invented. The address 東京都台東区浅草4-14-1 is consistently corroborated across multiple listing sites as being in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreet area, close to Sensō-ji, consistent with the target area description.

lead: 浅草 熟成味噌らーめん のりあき･美々
address_ja: 東京都台東区駒形1-9-9 川尻マンション1F

bio.background: The owner trained for roughly 17 years within the Keisuke (けいすけ) ramen group, one of Tokyo's well-known ramen brand families, before striking out on his own to open this small counter shop in Asakusa in April 2023.
bio.specialty: Jukusei Miso Ramen (熟成味噌らーめん) — a soup built from four types of aged Niigata miso and around 15 ingredients, served with medium-thick noodles and topped with chashu pork, a marinated soft-boiled egg, stir-fried vegetables, menma, back fat, ginger, and yuzu.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name, 美々 (Bibi), is described by food writers as expressing the owner's core wish for the food to be 'beautifully presented and deliciously eaten.'

anecdotes.text: Owner Noriaki Takeishi spent about 17 years training within the Keisuke ramen group before opening this independent shop in Asakusa in April 2023.

Diners are served a separate koji-miso paste made with monkfish (anglerfish) liver alongside the bowl, meant to be stirred in partway through the meal for an 'aji-hen' (taste-change) experience that shifts the soup's flavor as you eat.
The restaurant's name, 美々 (Bibi), is said to express the owner's wish for food that is 'beautifully presented and deliciously eaten.'

caveats: Hours/closed-day and precise coordinates could not be fully cross-verified: most directory listings (enjoytokyo.jp, sake-bon.com) state Sunday as the regular closing day, but at least one aggregated source described the shop as having no fixed closing day (無休/不定休) — confirm before a Sunday visit. Seat count varies slightly by source (counter of 6 plus one 2-person table vs. 'about 10 seats'); treated as a small, single-room counter shop either way. This is a traditional wheat-noodle miso ramen shop with no evidence of any gluten-free or vegan accommodation — flagged here only so celiac/vegan diners can safely skip it.
closed_days: Sunday (per most current directory listings), though some earlier write-ups described the shop as open daily with no fixed closing day — worth confirming if visiting on a Sunday.
cuisine: Miso Ramen (Jukusei / aged miso blend of four Niigata miso types)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering appears to go through a Japanese-language ticket machine with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff in any source reviewed; it's a short walk from Asakusa Station and draws some tourist foot traffic, but non-Japanese speakers should come with the dish name written out or a translation app.
geocode_note: The required GSI address-search endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and a fallback geocoder (Nominatim) both returned HTTP 403 in this environment — the destination hosts were blocked by the session's network egress policy, so coordinates could not be verified programmatically. Based on the address (Komagata 1-chome, Taito-ku) and multiple independent sources consistently describing the shop as a 1-2 minute walk from Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) and about a 5-minute walk from Sensoji, it plausibly sits well within the Sensoji target radius, but lat/lng are left null since they could not be confirmed via geocoding.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop. The noodles themselves contain gluten, and no source (official or diner coverage) mentions a gluten-free noodle substitute or a separate GF-safe preparation process. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30, Dinner 18:00-23:00, daily except Sunday (per most listings)
name_en: Asakusa Jukusei Miso Ramen Noriaki Bibi
name_ja: 浅草 熟成味噌らーめん のりあき・美々
neighborhood: Asakusa (Komagata), Taito-ku — near Asakusa Station (Ginza Line), about 5 minutes' walk from Sensoji

seats_text: Small counter shop — roughly 6 counter seats plus one 2-person table (estimates vary slightly by source, up to about 10 seats total)
vegan_detail: The broth is pork bone and chicken based, blended with miso, and standard toppings include pork chashu, pork back fat, and a soy-marinated egg. No plant-based broth or vegan/vegetarian menu option was found in any source covering the shop.

lead: 江戸もんじゃひょうたん

bio.background: Edo Monja Hyotan has run for roughly two and a half decades on a backstreet just off Nakamise-dori, a very short walk from Sensoji. The shop is styled to feel like an old Edo-period shophouse: guests slide open the front door and pass a nostalgic dagashi (penny-candy) corner before reaching the dining room and a tatami seating area upstairs.
bio.specialty: Mentai-mochi-cheese monjayaki (spicy cod roe, rice cake and cheese folded into the classic Tokyo-style griddled batter dish) is the shop's best-known order, alongside its "tokusen" (deluxe) monjayaki piled with mentaiko, shrimp, squid and mochi.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has appeared repeatedly on the TV program Docchi no Ryori Show and draws a steady stream of celebrity visitors.
Opening the sliding front door, visitors are greeted by an old-fashioned dagashi (penny-candy) counter before the dining area, giving the shop a nostalgic, time-slip feel.

caveats: Hours, the Tuesday closure pattern, and payment details were assembled from aggregator listings (Yahoo Map, EnjoyTokyo, Ekiten, gurunavi) rather than the restaurant's own site, since direct site fetches (including Tabelog itself and the GSI geocoder) were blocked by network egress policy in this session — only WebSearch snippets were available, and some sources gave conflicting hours (one listing says 11:00-21:00 vs. others' 11:00-next 5:00). Coordinates could not be verified. No vegan or gluten-free accommodation was found in any source; the GF/vegan assessment here is reasoned from standard monjayaki composition (wheat batter, likely bonito dashi) rather than a shop-specific statement.
closed_days: Tuesdays, with exceptions — open on 2nd/4th Tuesdays, national holidays, throughout August, and during the New Year period
cuisine: もんじゃ焼き (Monjayaki / Tokyo-style griddled batter dish)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the shop sits right in tourist Asakusa near Sensoji, but expect a lively, mostly Japanese-speaking room typical of a local monjayaki parlor rather than an English-fluent staff.
gf_detail: Monjayaki batter is fundamentally wheat-flour based, mixed into a dashi stock and cooked with soy sauce and Worcestershire-style sauces that typically contain wheat. No rice-flour or gluten-free batter substitute, dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated cooking surface, or allergen protocol was found for this shop in any source, so it should not be considered safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and every direct restaurant-site fetch (Tabelog, Ekiten, EnjoyTokyo, Retty, gurunavi, Asakusa Noren, etc.) returned HTTP 403 in this session due to network egress policy, so coordinates could not be verified via geocoder. No third-party listing surfaced explicit lat/lng either. Multiple independent listings consistently place the shop about a 1-minute walk from Sensoji and roughly 2 minutes from Asakusa Station Exit 6, which is well within the Sensoji 0.8 km radius, but I could not confirm a precise coordinate pair, so lat/lng are left null rather than estimated.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00–5:00 the following morning (L.O. 4:00); Saturdays, Sundays and holidays 10:00–5:00 the following morning (L.O. 4:00). One aggregator listing instead shows 11:00–21:00, so hours should be reconfirmed before a visit.
name_ja: 江戸もんじゃひょうたん
seats_text: Roughly 100 seats, with ground-floor tables and a tatami seating area on the second floor
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan-specific accommodation was found for this shop. A diner could in principle ask for a vegetable-only monjayaki (cabbage, corn, mochi, cheese) omitting seafood/meat toppings, but standard monjayaki broth and the Worcestershire-style sauce used at Tokyo monjayaki shops are typically bonito/fish-based, and this has not been confirmed either way here — vegans should ask staff directly about the dashi and sauce before ordering.

roles: Historic sukiyaki (wagyu beef hot pot) restaurant founded in 1880
Reopened in 2022 under WDI Group with the former proprietor and longtime staff involved
bio.background: Chinya traces its roots to the Edo period, when the shop's predecessor was known locally as 'Chin-ya' because it kept and supplied prized 'chin' (Japanese Chin) lapdogs to daimyo lords and wealthy merchants, and also served as their veterinarian — the unusual name stuck even after the family opened it as a restaurant in 1880 (Meiji 13). It became a dedicated sukiyaki specialty shop in 1903 and grew into one of Asakusa's oldest and most storied sukiyaki houses. The aging Meiji-era building, worn down further by pandemic-era business struggles, closed in August 2021. WDI Group stepped in to preserve the Chinya name and traditions, reopening the restaurant in spring 2022 at a new address in Hanakawado overlooking the Sumida River, with former proprietor Fumihiko Sumiyoshi and former staff members continuing to be involved in day-to-day operations.
bio.specialty: Sukiyaki made with 'tekisashi-niku' (適サシ肉) — female Japanese Black Wagyu beef raised for around 30 months from carcasses under 480kg, chosen for a balance of marbling and lean meat, then aged to concentrate flavor. It is cooked tableside in a copper pot with a traditional sweetened soy-sauce warishita and dipped in raw egg.
bio.philosophy: Carrying more than 140 years of sukiyaki tradition, craftsmanship and flavor forward for new generations, as expressed on the restaurant's own site: preserving 'its tradition, its dedication and its taste for the future and for the world.'

anecdotes.text: Before it was a restaurant, the shop was known in the Edo period as 'Chin-ya' because it kept and supplied treasured 'chin' lapdogs to daimyo lords and wealthy merchants, and also acted as their veterinarian — the name carried over when the family became restaurateurs in 1880.

When the original Meiji-era building could no longer be maintained and the pandemic hit business hard, Chinya closed in August 2021; WDI Group then preserved the brand and reopened it in March 2022 on the banks of the Sumida River in Hanakawado, with the former proprietor and former staff members returning to work there.

caveats: Reopened in March 2022 at a new address in Hanakawado (still central Asakusa, near the Sumida River) after the original Edo-dori shop closed in August 2021; it is now run under WDI Group's corporate restaurant management, with the former owner and staff involved, rather than as an independent family business. Pricing is high-end (roughly ¥9,000 average for lunch, ¥13,000 for dinner, plus a 10% service charge and a ¥4,000 private-room charge). There is no gluten-free or vegan menu; the dish is built around wagyu beef, a soy-sauce-based warishita sauce, and raw egg dipping, so it is not a safe choice for celiac or vegan diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant. Exact GPS coordinates could not be programmatically geocoded in this session (all external geocoding/mapping hosts, including the GSI address-search API, were blocked by network policy) — see geocode_note. The confirmed street address and multiple listings placing it ~438m from Asakusa Station indicate it is well within the Sensōji/Asakusa target radius.
closed_days: None (open daily / 無休 per restaurant listings)
cuisine: Sukiyaki (wagyu beef hot pot); shabu-shabu also served
cultural_comfort_note: Chinya maintains a full English website with an English-language history/story section and menu PDF, plus English-friendly online reservation platforms (TableCheck, Tabelog), making it comfortable to book and dine at without Japanese ability.
geocode_note: All external geocoding services (GSI's msearch.gsi.go.jp address-search API, OpenStreetMap Nominatim) and most restaurant/map source pages returned network-policy 403s in this session, so coordinates could not be verified programmatically. gf_detail: The sukiyaki warishita (sweetened soy-sauce simmering sauce) is made with standard Japanese shoyu, which is brewed from wheat; no gluten-free menu, dedicated gluten-free preparation, or allergen accommodations are advertised anywhere in official or listing sources. There is no evidence of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol. This should not be treated as celiac-safe without direct, advance confirmation by phone (03-3841-0010), and even then cross-contact risk in a shared kitchen built around soy-sauce-based cooking is likely.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:00), Dinner 16:30–21:30 (L.O. 20:00, drinks L.O. 20:30). Sat/Sun/Holidays: 11:00–21:30 (L.O. 20:00, drinks L.O. 20:30). Open daily.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sukiyaki at Chinya is cooked in a traditional warishita (sweetened soy-sauce sauce); standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, and no wheat-free or tamari-based alternative is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core dish is built around wagyu beef cooked in a shared pot with a soy-based sauce and finished with a raw-egg dip, so there is no realistic way to keep a vegan order separate from meat-based cooking.

seats_text: Approximately 74 seats total, including three private rooms (7, 6, and 8 seats).
vegan_detail: Chinya is a wagyu beef sukiyaki/shabu-shabu specialty restaurant; the signature preparation involves meat simmered in a soy-based warishita and dipped in raw egg. No vegan menu, plant-based sukiyaki option, or vegetarian course is advertised on the official site or major reservation platforms.

bio.background: Opened in March 2021 a short walk from Asakusa's Kaminarimon Gate, the cafe pairs an in-house roaster with Japanese-Western fusion desserts.
bio.philosophy: The shop is named for Kukurihime-no-Kami (菊理媛), a deity of ties and reconciliation from the Nihon Shoki, chosen with the wish that connections made here would spread outward from Asakusa to Japan and the world. The brand mark is five overlapping circles standing for en (縁, bonds) that form five petals where they meet, and the coffee blends reference the 24 sekki (solar terms) of the traditional calendar as a wish for customers' wellbeing.
bio.specialty: Seasonal wagashi-style fruit parfaits (和パフェ) built around single-origin, in-house roasted coffee (Colombian, Honduran and Ethiopian beans roasted to each bean's character) plus original nut butters and coffee confitures.

anecdotes.text: The shop's own 'Our Thoughts' page lays out the Kukurihime naming story and the five-circle en (connection) symbolism behind the coffee blends.
A branding case study describes the shop's identity design, built around the five-petal 'en' motif and the in-house roaster's bean selection.
A travel feature describes visiting for a fruit parfait near Sensoji and 'feeling en (connection)' in the seasonal dessert.

caveats: No page could be fetched directly (Tabelog, the official site, and most Japanese food sites returned network/policy 403s in this session); every fact below comes from Google/search-result snippets of those pages rather than a direct page read, so treat wording as paraphrased, not quoted. No gluten-free or vegan-specific claims were found anywhere for this shop — it reads as a standard (if well-branded) coffee-and-parfait cafe, not a dietary-focused one. Payment methods (cash-only or not) were not explicitly confirmed; 'false' is inferred from the shop's use of online reservation/e-commerce platforms, not a direct statement. Coordinates are an estimate (GSI geocoder was unreachable), not a verified geocode. Seat count is not published anywhere found.

cultural_comfort_note: A polished, Instagram/tourist-friendly cafe near Kaminarimon with online booking via multiple platforms, but no confirmed English menu — basic Japanese or pointing at photos should get you through ordering.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, rice-flour claim, or allergy-handling statement was found in any source. Straight coffee is naturally gluten-free, but the signature parfaits layer in cake, cookie, and other wheat-based components as a matter of course, and there is no stated separation of prep areas or ingredients. Treat this as a normal cafe for GF purposes — ask staff item-by-item and do not assume any dessert is safe without checking.

neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku — about 1 minute's walk from Kaminarimon Gate
seats_text: Counter and round-table seating (exact seat count not published in any source found)

The signature parfaits are built on cream, ice cream and other dairy components typical of the genre, with no indication of plant-based substitutes. Black coffee itself is vegan, but do not assume any parfait or sweet is dairy-free without asking, and there's no confirmation on hidden bonito/dashi in any savory items (the menu appears to be coffee and dessert-focused, so this risk is likely low but unconfirmed).

bio.background: Fuji Ramen was opened by a chef who previously worked at Yoroiya, a well-known long-established ramen shop in Asakusa, before starting his own counter-only shop nearby on Kokusai-dori.
bio.specialty: Tonkotsu ramen topped with cabbage and mayu (burnt garlic oil) — a distinctive combination for a pork-bone broth shop; the shop also offers tsukemen (dipping noodles).
bio.philosophy: The shop is built around self-made noodles, made with close attention to the wheat used, paired with a rich, slow-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood broth.

anecdotes.text: 富士らーめんは浅草で有名なラーメン屋『与ろゐ屋』出身の方が開業されたお店 (Fuji Ramen was opened by someone who came from Yoroiya, a famous Asakusa ramen shop).
小麦からこだわった自家製麺のらーめんのお店で、豚骨系濃厚スープが売り (A shop built on self-made noodles that are particular about the wheat used, with a rich tonkotsu-style broth as its selling point).

No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated GF preparation, or wheat-free broth/seasoning option is documented anywhere in available sources. Standard ramen shoyu tare and broths in this style typically contain wheat-derived soy sauce. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

vegan_detail: The house specialty is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth combined with seafood (魚介) stock; no vegetarian or vegan menu items, plant-based broth, or dashi-free option is documented in any available source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

roles: Founded by Sadakichi (定吉), a native of Mikawa Province (present-day Aichi Prefecture) who came to Edo and first worked in the egg-distribution trade before turning to tempura

anecdotes.text: The shop is immortalized in the old Asakusa saying "first Asakusa, second Kannon, third Sansada's tempura" (一に浅草、二に観音、三に三定の天ぷら), placing it alongside Sensō-ji itself as a must-see of the neighborhood.

Founder Sadakichi originally battered small fish and shellfish caught in the waters near Edo and deep-fried them in sesame oil at a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō district — the beginning of what became Sansada; the shop's name is a contraction of "Mikawaya Sadakichi" (三河屋定吉).

The restaurant has never opened branch locations, said to be rooted in the philosophy that the owner should never leave the shop; today it occupies only its original storefront on Kaminarimon-dōri plus an adjoining building.

bio.background: Sansada traces its origin to around 1831, when Sadakichi began selling Edo-mae fish and shellfish tempura from a street stall in Nihonbashi's Ningyōchō. The business later moved to its present spot immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate in Asakusa and, founded in 1837 (Tenpō 8), is considered the oldest continuously operating tempura restaurant in Japan.
bio.origin: Mikawa Province (present-day Aichi Prefecture) via Nihonbashi, Edo
bio.philosophy: A single-storefront philosophy — no branches, ever — built on nearly two centuries of frying Edo-mae tempura in sesame oil at the same spot beside Kaminarimon Gate.
bio.specialty: Kakiage (mixed tempura of small Edo-mae fish and shellfish, offered in large/medium/small sizes) and the tendon rice bowl, both fried in aromatic sesame oil in the traditional Edo style.

caveats: Geocoding could not be completed: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by this session's network egress policy (403), so lat/lng are left null rather than estimated — the address (immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate) is well within the Sensō-ji/Asakusa target radius based on textual descriptions from multiple sources. closed_days: Irregular closures, approximately twice a year (定休日は不定期で年2回程度)
cuisine: 天ぷら（江戸前) — traditional Edo-style tempura
cultural_comfort_note: Sits directly beside Kaminarimon Gate and serves huge numbers of tourists, so staff are used to non-Japanese-speaking guests and a laminated English menu is available on request, though the main menu and ordering are still primarily in Japanese.

Address is corroborated by multiple sources as immediately east of Kaminarimon Gate, well within the Sensō-ji/Asakusa 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: Tempura batter follows the traditional Edo-style wheat-flour recipe, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat) — no gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific menu is documented on the official site or in press/tourism coverage. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct advance confirmation by phone (03-3841-3200), and even then a safe outcome is unlikely given the shared-oil, wheat-batter format.
name_ja: 雷門 三定（さんさだ）
seats_text: Main building on Kaminarimon-dōri plus an adjoining new building; the new building alone has two small private rooms and two banquet halls seating up to 80 people, but no total seat count for the whole restaurant is published.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Edo-mae seafood tempura (kakiage, shrimp, fish) fried in shared sesame oil, and the traditional tentsuyu dipping sauce is typically dashi-based (often including bonito/katsuo). No vegan menu, vegetable-only tempura set, or dashi substitution is documented on the official site or in press coverage, so vegan and vegetarian diners would need to ask staff directly about ingredient substitutions and shared-fryer cross-contact.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — founded in 1968 (Showa 43)
bio.specialty: 豆かんてん (mame-kanten) — a simple dessert of slow-simmered red peas, kanten agar jelly, and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup)
bio.background: Umemura opened in Asakusa's 'ura-Asakusa' backstreets (just behind Sensō-ji) in 1968 and is widely credited by Tokyo food writers as the birthplace of mame-kan / mame-kanten, the bean-and-agar sweet that later spread to sweets shops across the city. The house version starts with Hokkaido red peas (aka-endou) simmered for around five hours until tender, served over firm-textured kanten agar with a pour of kuromitsu syrup. The shop is now run by the second generation of the founding family, who has kept the original recipe and method largely unchanged.
bio.philosophy: Continuity of the original 1968 recipe and technique, passed down within the family rather than modernized or expanded into a chain.

anecdotes.text: The shop and its mame-kanten have been featured in the gourmet manga/TV drama Kodoku no Gurume (Solitary Gourmet), which helped cement its reputation as the 'birthplace' of mame-kan.
Described as the 'originator' (発祥) of mame-kan in Asakusa, with beans cooked roughly five hours before serving.

Coordinates are therefore left null. Based on the confirmed address (Taito-ku, Asakusa 3-chōme 22-12), the shop sits in the backstreets directly behind Sensō-ji ('ura-Asakusa'), which is well within the 0.8km Sensō-ji target radius (35.7148, 139.7967).
gf_detail: The signature mame-kanten is built from red peas, kanten (agar) jelly, and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup) — none of these are wheat-derived ingredients, so the core dish appears naturally free of wheat by composition. However, no dedicated gluten-free protocol, ingredient labeling, or staff allergy-handling statement from the shop was found, and other menu items (dressings, tokoroten with tare sauce, etc.) were not individually verified for hidden soy-sauce or wheat-based seasonings. Celiac diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering.

positives.text: The shop's signature mame-kanten is made from a short, identifiable ingredient list — Hokkaido red peas simmered about five hours, kanten agar, and kuromitsu syrup — with no meat, fish, or dashi in the base recipe.

seats_text: Small, traditional shop-front seating; exact seat count not confirmed from available sources.
vegan_detail: The signature mame-kanten (red peas, kanten agar, kuromitsu black-sugar syrup) appears plant-based by ingredient composition, with no dairy, egg, or dashi evident in that specific dish. However, several other listed menu items — cream anmitsu (クリームあんみつ) and cream mitsumame (クリームみつ豆) — explicitly contain dairy cream, so the menu overall is mixed rather than vegan-friendly across the board. No staff confirmation of hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., gelatin, bonito-based flavoring) was found, so diners should ask before ordering.

lead: 浅草名代らーめん 与ろゐ屋

bio.background: The founder previously ran several ramen shops in Chiyoda and elsewhere before deciding to recreate, in Asakusa, the taste of a bowl of "chuka soba" he remembered from a soba shop near his childhood home — one whose broth carried a subtle aroma of dried sardines and bonito rather than the heavier style typical of Chinese-style ramen restaurants. He opened Yoroiya on Denboin-dori in July 1992. The shop's name traces back to his father's menswear shop, called "Kairoiya."
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Wafu (Japanese-style) shoyu ramen: a broth built from pork bone and chicken carcass, finished with carefully selected niboshi (dried sardines), katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and kombu, with a light touch of yuzu aroma. The shop also serves a morning ramen (asa-ra) service from 8:30am, and gyoza made with garlic-free Oyama chicken thigh and harusame glass noodles instead of pork.
bio.philosophy: Recreate the gentle, dashi-forward chuka soba flavor of old Asakusa/Tokyo soba-ya cooking rather than the richer tonkotsu or heavily seasoned styles that became common elsewhere, and keep that flavor consistent for the neighborhood's regulars as well as visitors.

anecdotes.text: The owner opened his first ramen shop in Chiyoda Ward and ran several others before returning to Asakusa in 1992 to recreate the ramen he remembered from a soba shop near his childhood home.
The shop has been loved by local residents for roughly three decades and has been inducted into the 'Hall of Fame' of Ishigami-bon, the annual ramen guidebook published since 1998.

caveats: Coordinates below are unavailable, not approximated. The GSI address-search API and all direct site fetches were unreachable from this session (network/proxy policy blocked outbound connections to yoroiya.jp, tabelog.com, msearch.gsi.go.jp, geocoding.jp, and every other destination tried, including unrelated domains like wikipedia.org — only the web-search tool returned data, via cached snippets). All facts here come from search-engine snippets of the official site (yoroiya.jp) and independent sources (Tabelog, asakusa.gr.jp, karakida.co.jp, local blogs) rather than direct page reads. Multiple independent sources agree on the core facts (address, hours, 1992 founding, vegan ramen item), giving reasonable confidence, but exact menu wording/prices should be treated as approximate.
closed_days: None stated — open year-round (年中無休), aside from occasional one-off maintenance closures announced on the shop's site/social media.
cuisine: Ramen (Edo/wafu-style chuka soba — pork bone and chicken broth finished with niboshi, katsuobushi and kombu)
cultural_comfort_note: A one-minute walk from Sensoji and hugely popular with foreign visitors (long lunchtime queues of overseas tourists are reported); the shop keeps English and simplified-Chinese menus for non-Japanese-speaking guests.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle or soy-sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere, including on the vegan ramen. The noodles are standard wheat chuka-soba noodles (the vegan version is described only as "egg-free," not wheat-free), the tare is a wafu shoyu (soy sauce) base which in Japanese ramen shops is almost always wheat-containing, and the gyoza wrapper is also wheat-based. There is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free preparation area. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should treat this as not a safe option.
