bio.background: A small husband-and-wife-run yakitori counter that opened in March 2021 in Nishi-Asakusa, a short walk from the Asakusa View Hotel. The shop occupies a renovated space that was formerly a tendon (rice-bowl) restaurant; the owners kept a sense of the building's history while giving it a clean, updated feel. It is run by just two people, the owner and a woman working the counter alongside him.
bio.specialty: Skewers grilled over charcoal, including a raw-wasabi semi-seared chicken breast (namawasabi no hannama sasami) and skewers such as harami (skirt) and shiitake mushroom that reviewers single out; a set course (salad, five yakitori skewers, rice bowl, and dessert) is offered for about 3,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened in March 2021 in a renovated building that used to house a tendon shop, with the owners keeping traces of the older building's character while making it feel fresh.
Diners highlight the harami and shiitake skewers as standouts, and the 3,000-yen course (salad, five grilled skewers, rice bowl, dessert) as good value.

caveats: Verified only via aggregator listings (Tabelog, Gnavi, Retty, Hitosara) reached through search snippets — direct page fetches (Tabelog, Retty, Hitosara, Gnavi, Google Maps, and the GSI geocoder) all failed with proxy-level 403 errors in this session, so nothing here was confirmed by directly reading the source pages, only by search-engine summaries of them. Vegans should ask specifically about the shiitake skewer's seasoning and whether any dashi or animal stock touches the vegetable dishes.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門２丁目７－６ 豊田ビル１F（〒111-0034）

bio.origin: Shandong-style Chinese hand-pulled noodle (拉麺) tradition, brought to Asakusa in 1977
bio.specialty: Hand-pulled noodles (手打ち麺) — stretched and slapped by hand in the shop's street-facing window and used across the menu: shoyu ramen, tantanmen, tsukemen, and hand-made gyoza
bio.philosophy: Noodles are pulled to order only after a customer orders, so the kitchen never holds pre-made noodles; the shop treats the hand-pulling itself as a craft, reportedly taking a noodle-puller at least two years to master.
bio.background: Opened in Asakusa in 1977 (Showa 52), a short walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate, as a Chinese-style hand-pulled noodle specialist. It later added a second location in Nippori, but the Asakusa shop remains the original.

anecdotes.text: The percussive slap of the noodle-maker's hands hitting the dough echoes out through a window facing the street, so passersby on the way to Sensoji can watch the noodles being hand-pulled before they're even cooked.

One diner wrote about having craved the shop's hand-pulled noodles for 15 years before finally trying the tsukemen, topped with scallions, bean sprouts, chashu, nori and menma, describing a distinctive chewy, almost 'sliding' texture unlike ordinary ramen noodles.

roles: noodle-pulling craftspeople (職人) who train for at least two years to master the technique

caveats: This shop's entire identity is built on hand-pulled wheat noodles, so it is not a fit for celiac or strict gluten-free diners, and no vegan option was found (the broth is pork-bone-and-chicken based). It is included for its genuine local-institution character, not as a GF/vegan-safe pick — treat it as sightseeing/flavor-only unless you can eat wheat and meat broth freely. Exact payment methods (cash vs. card/e-money) and whether reservations are accepted could not be confirmed from available sources; call the shop (03-3841-6002) to check before visiting.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-21-3 エリカビル 2F

bio.chef_name: 板長マウンさん (Maung) and 板前ニイさん (Ni)
bio.origin: Myanmar
bio.background: Head chef Maung came to Japan in 1996, during a period when Myanmar's military government was cracking down on the democracy movement and the economy was struggling, and spent 24 years training as a sushi craftsman in Japan. Fellow chef Ni has close to 20 years of sushi experience in Japan. In June 2019 (the first year of the Reiwa era), a group of Myanmar-born residents living in Japan pooled their own money to set up a company together and open this sushi restaurant in Asakusa, naming it after the new era.
bio.philosophy: To show that decades of hard-won craft — not nationality — is what makes a sushi chef, after facing skepticism from some early customers about non-Japanese chefs behind the counter.
bio.specialty: Traditional Edomae-style sushi alongside original creations, including a spicy roll and the restaurant's signature 'Asakusa roll' (浅草巻き).

anecdotes.text: A magazine feature's headline recounts a customer's blunt first reaction on seeing the chefs — 'Why is a foreigner making this?!' — framed as the starting point of the chefs' effort to win diners over through skill.

The restaurant opened in June 2019 after Myanmar-born residents in Japan pooled their own funds to establish a company together, and the shop later ran a crowdfunding campaign on READYFOR to support its operations.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902) as a Japanese restaurant specializing in fugu (pufferfish) cuisine in Asakusa. The restaurant has operated continuously for over 120 years, weathering the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and the aftermath of WWII in 1945. It is now run by a third-generation owner who was born and raised in Asakusa. The building was renovated in September 2016, with horigotatsu (sunken-kotatsu) seating added to both the first and second floors.
bio.specialty: Fugu kaiseki course centered on tessa (fugu sashimi), techiri (fugu hot pot), nikogori (jellied fugu broth), karaage (fried fugu), hire-zake (fin sake), and shirako (fugu milt) — the grilled shirako, in season from around November, is especially prized by regulars.

anecdotes.text: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902), the restaurant has continued as a Japanese restaurant ever since, passing through three generations of ownership and surviving the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and the end of WWII in 1945.
The restaurant sits about a 1-minute walk from Kaminarimon Gate and roughly 3 minutes from Asakusa Station, tucked in the backstreets near Sensoji.
It was renovated in September 2016 (Heisei 28), with seating on both the first and second floors converted to horigotatsu sunken-kotatsu style.

seats_text: Roughly 17 tables / about 50 seats, with horigotatsu (sunken-kotatsu) seating on both the first and second floors.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around fugu (pufferfish): sashimi, hot pot, fried fugu, fin sake, and milt. There are no plant-based or vegetarian dishes evident on the menu from any source, and as a single-species fish specialty restaurant it is not set up to accommodate vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-42-12 浅草ダイカンプラザ天仁ビル1F

bio.background: Chef Oku opened this nine-seat counter in Asakusa in April 2021. He trained previously at the respected Edomae sushi restaurant Sushi Hashiguchi (鮨 橋口), and the shop is often described by reviewers as carrying on the lineage/style of the well-regarded 'Takase' (高勢) school of Edomae sushi.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi omakase, with particular attention to knife work, cutting thickness, and rice/vinegar balance
bio.philosophy: Adherence to classic Edomae sushi fundamentals, with the chef's individual sensibility expressed through small technical details rather than novelty.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a calm, personable nine-seat counter where the chef looks after regulars closely, giving the room a warm, low-key atmosphere rather than a formal one.
A sushi-focused blog profiled Oku as a lesser-known but highly skilled craftsman ('知る人ぞ知る腕利き職人') carrying on the Takase lineage in Asakusa.

caveats: Closing times were not published on any source found (only opening/seating times), so the listed end-of-service times are a reasonable estimate for a single-seating omakase, not a confirmed figure. Coordinates could not be geocoded in this session (geocoding services were unreachable); location confidence relies on the published address and station-walk description rather than a verified lat/lng.
closed_days: Sunday (some listings also note irregular/holiday closures — best to confirm when booking)
cuisine: 寿司 (Edomae sushi omakase)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, intimate Japanese sushi-ya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service; reservations are typically made by phone or Japanese reservation platforms, so first-time foreign visitors may want to book through a concierge or hotel.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or celiac-specific accommodation is advertised anywhere found. Traditional Edomae sushi relies on wheat-containing soy sauce (nikiri) brushed onto nigiri and may include tempura or other wheat-based elements within the omakase course. Celiac diners should call ahead (in Japanese, or via a Japanese-speaking concierge) to ask whether the chef can substitute tamari or omit sauced/fried items — no such accommodation could be confirmed from available sources.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri: dinner seating from 18:30. Wed, Sat: lunch seating from 12:00 and dinner seating from 18:30. Closed Sunday. Exact closing times not published (single-seating omakase course); one source separately listed evening hours as "from 17:00," so evening start time may vary by day or have changed — confirm when booking.
geocode_note: Both the GSI address-search API and a fallback geocoder (Nominatim) were unreachable from this environment (network/proxy blocked all requests to those hosts). Based on the published address (Asakusa 3-chome, described as about a 6-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station Exit A2, near Sensō-ji), the location is very likely within Asakusa proper and close to the Sensō-ji target radius, but exact coordinates could not be verified.
seats_text: Nine-seat wooden counter, chef's omakase only
vegan_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi omakase built around raw and cooked seafood; no vegetarian or vegan tasting menu is offered or advertised, and the fixed omakase course is not structured to be adapted to a plant-based diet.

bio.origin: Grew up in the ramen scene around Fujimaru's Nishiarai Daishi branch, a shop in the Ramen Jiro lineage
bio.background: Owner Kimura trained at Ramen Fujimaru's Nishiarai Daishi location, a shop descended from the Ramen Jiro style, before opening the original 自家製麺NO11 in Itabashi (Ooyama/Shimoitabashi). In April 2025 he opened this second location on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, taking over a space that had previously housed 麺屋まるいち, a shop No11 had also produced.
bio.specialty: Jiro-style pork ramen built on housemade extra-thick noodles and an emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth, topped with tender braised pork, soft-boiled 'kuta' vegetables, garlic and back fat.

anecdotes.text: Food blogger Miku Okazaki covered the Asakusa opening and interviewed owner Kimura about his approach to the noodles and broth for the shop's 'makanai interview' feature, noting a congratulatory flower display in the shop window marking the opening.
Tokyo ramen blog Ramental described the shop as drawing a constant queue and praised the sharp, clean cut of the emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth.

caveats: This is a Jiro-style pork ramen shop centered on housemade wheat noodles and a pork-bone soy-sauce broth — it has no gluten-free or vegan orientation and was included in the sweep as a general omnivore ramen spot near Asakusa, not as a dietary-safe recommendation. Exact GPS coordinates could not be verified because geocoding APIs were unreachable in this session; proximity to Sensoji is inferred from published walking directions, not measured.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day found in any source. Like many Jiro-style shops, it may close early or skip a day if the housemade noodles or broth sell out — check the shop's X/Twitter account (@no11_asakusa) for same-day notices.
cuisine: Ramen (Jiro-style / 二郎系), housemade noodles
cultural_comfort_note: Orders go through a cash-only ticket vending machine, and Jiro-style etiquette means calling out topping preferences (vegetables, garlic, extra fat) when your bowl is called — a little Japanese vocabulary makes the ritual far less intimidating for first-time visitors.
geocode_note: GSI (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and OpenStreetMap Nominatim geocoding requests were both blocked by the session's network egress policy (403 on every attempt), so coordinates could not be programmatically verified. Multiple independent sources consistently describe the address as being on Hisago-dori shopping street, about a 3-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station and a 12-minute walk from the Ginza Line's Asakusa Station — placing it roughly a few hundred meters west of Sensoji Temple and very likely inside the 0.8km radius — but this was not independently geocoded, so lat/lng are left null rather than estimated.
gf_detail: This is a Jiro-inspired ramen shop built entirely around housemade thick wheat noodles and an emulsified pork-bone shoyu (soy sauce) broth. No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or any celiac-safe alternative is mentioned anywhere in the shop's own posts or in any review found. Not a safe option for anyone avoiding gluten.
hours_raw: Multiple sources (the shop's own X/Twitter posts, ramen blogs) report Sun–Thu hours of 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), though one listing gives the evening opening as 17:30 rather than 17:00. Friday/Saturday hours were not confirmed in any source found; the same window is assumed here but is unverified.
neighborhood: Asakusa (Hisago-dori), near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) based, emulsified pork-bone soup; no wheat-free soy sauce, tamari, or gluten-free broth alternative is described in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is pork-bone based and every bowl comes with pork toppings by default; no vegetarian or vegan broth or topping substitution appears in the menu as described by reviewers (Ramen: ¥1,200, Pork Ramen: ¥1,600).

seats_text: A small facing counter, reported as about 9 seats (two rows of 5 and 4), with a couple of additional outdoor terrace seats; exact counts vary slightly between sources.
vegan_detail: The menu is centered on pork-bone soup and pork toppings (chāshū pork, back fat), and no plant-based broth, noodle, or topping option was found in any review, blog coverage, or the shop's own social posts. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸1-15-4 アークノア1F

bio.background: 竹三郎(Takesaburo)は東武線・浅草駅から徒歩2〜3分、馬道通り沿いにある濃厚豚骨（家系）ラーメン店で、2013年12月23日に開業した。浅草寺にほど近い立地で、地元ブログでは「浅草寺にもっとも近い家系」として紹介されている。
bio.origin: Opened December 23, 2013 in Hanakawado, Asakusa, along Umamichi-dori, a short walk from Sensoji.
bio.specialty: 満州トロトロ担々汁あり油そば（ゴマだれ入りの担々風あぶらそば）と、同じ具でコチジャンを効かせた辛口の「汁あり油そば 燃えよドラゴン」。鉄鍋の余熱でスープが煮詰まりソース状になって極太麺に絡むのが特徴。濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメンも看板メニュー。
roles: ramen shop / 濃厚豚骨・家系ラーメン専門店

anecdotes.text: 浅草寺にもっとも近い家系〜竹三郎＠浅草 (blog post noting the shop's proximity to Sensoji temple)
Rich tonkotsu-shoyu broth described as thick yet mellow, with thick straight noodles that are chewy and smooth.

caveats: Direct page fetches (Tabelog, official sources, GSI geocoder, Yahoo/Retty/Hotpepper) all returned HTTP 403 in this session due to a network/proxy restriction affecting the WebFetch tool and outbound curl for this host; all facts below come from WebSearch's own page retrieval/summarization rather than a direct fetch I could inspect line-by-line. No gluten-free or vegan information was found anywhere in available sources; this is a standard wheat-noodle, pork-bone-broth, soy-sauce-based ramen shop with no indication of dietary accommodation, so it is a poor fit for a GF/vegan-aware app despite meeting the location/existence bar.
closed_days: 月曜日 (Monday)
cuisine: 豚骨ラーメン・アブラソバ（家系ラーメン）
cultural_comfort_note: Neighborhood-style Japanese ramen counter near Asakusa Station with no confirmed English menu, English signage, or English-speaking staff in any available source, so basic Japanese or pointing at photos may be needed to order.

Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners based on available information.

Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-6-7 東京楽天地浅草ビル4F（浅草横町内）

roles: Eel (unagi) specialty izakaya, one of several branches of the いづも eel-skewer concept
bio.background: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the Tokyo Rakutenchi Asakusa Building.
bio.origin: Osaka (Fukushima)
bio.specialty: Unagi tamadon and unagi gyudon (eel rice bowls with egg or beef), unagi kamahoyaki (semi-grilled eel fillet), and eel kushiyaki skewers, served alongside grilled beef tongue, yakitori, and horse sashimi.
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated aim is to make traditionally pricey, formal unagi more affordable and approachable by serving it izakaya-style in a lively, casual setting.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka honten relocated and relaunched with new menu items in 2024, described as an effort to carry Japanese eel food culture forward while keeping it reasonably priced ('大阪一リーズナブルな鰻屋').
The Asakusa branch sits inside 'Asakusa Yokocho,' a food-and-festival themed dining hall built to let visitors experience Japanese festival culture alongside food from multiple genres.

caveats: Network conditions this session blocked WebFetch on nearly every domain (Tabelog, official sites, even the government geocoder), so this record was built from WebSearch result snippets/summaries rather than directly fetched page bodies. Hours, seat count, and coordinates come from secondary aggregator listings that partly disagree with each other (see hours_status and geocode_note) and should be spot-checked before publishing. No gluten-free or vegan information could be found for this specific branch — it appears to be a straightforward eel/meat izakaya with no dietary-accommodation signal either way.
closed_days: Reported as irregular closures / rarely closed (不定休、ほぼ無休) — no fixed weekly closed day found.
cuisine: うなぎ（蒲の穂焼き）・牛タン・焼鳥・馬刺し
cultural_comfort_note: Sits inside a tourist-oriented "food and festival" dining hall a minute from Asakusa Station with an English Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

As a traditional unagi/izakaya menu, the eel glaze (tare) and dipping sauces are almost certainly soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). Treat as unsafe for celiac diners absent direct confirmation from staff.

This should be re-verified against the official geocoder when network access is restored.

name_ja: うなぎの蒲の穂焼 牛タン 焼鳥 馬刺し いづも 浅草横町

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Tsukushi is a long-running monjayaki (and okonomiyaki) restaurant on Asakusa's Hoppy-dōri (Hoppy Street), tucked behind the Denbō-in temple grounds just west of Sensō-ji. It is roughly 55 years old and is often described as the second-oldest monjayaki shop in the neighborhood, having kept its core recipe essentially unchanged for more than 40 years. It draws a mix of shitamachi (old-downtown) regulars, weekend queues, and a following of visiting celebrities.
bio.specialty: Monjayaki and okonomiyaki — notably the loaded 'Deluxe' monja topped with pork, beef, squid, shrimp, and a raw egg — alongside 40-plus menu items that include simmered beef tendon (gyū-suji nikomi) and various butter-grilled dishes.
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes continuity: staff describe preserving the same taste and recipes they've served for over 40 years rather than chasing novelty, treating monjayaki as a genuine old-Tokyo comfort food.

anecdotes.text: The menu reportedly includes items created with input from celebrity regulars, including dishes nicknamed 'Gackt' and 'Chō-tokkyū (Super Express) monja.'

The restaurant sits on Hoppy-dōri, Asakusa's nostalgic strip of open-air izakaya stalls just behind Sensō-ji's Denbō-in temple grounds, an area long associated with Asakusa's downtown drinking-and-eating culture.

Monjayaki itself began as a favorite childhood snack in downtown Tokyo, especially around Asakusa, where children cooked it on small iron plates at candy stores in the 1950s–60s — the tradition Tsukushi continues today.

caveats: Payment method acceptance (cash-only vs. card) could not be confirmed from any accessible source this session, so cash_only is a conservative default, not a verified fact — confirm on arrival. Exact coordinates could not be geocoded: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable from this session due to a network/proxy block (all requests to the gsi.go.jp domain returned connection failures), not because the address didn't resolve. Multiple independent sources (Hotpepper, Jalan, local Asakusa blogs) consistently place the address on Hoppy-dōri, roughly a 2-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station and well within the target radius of Sensō-ji, but lat/lng is unconfirmed and left null. This is a traditional wheat-flour-batter monjayaki/okonomiyaki restaurant with no advertised gluten-free or vegan accommodations — treat GF/vegan info as a caution, not an endorsement. Most detail-page sources (Tabelog, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, Ekiten, Enjoytokyo, Tripadvisor) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch this session; facts below are corroborated via search-result synthesis across multiple of these listings rather than a single direct page fetch, so treat operational specifics (hours/seats) as reasonably but not maximally certain.
closed_days: Tuesdays (the closed day can shift week to week — call ahead before a Tuesday visit)
cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き (Monjayaki & Okonomiyaki)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is reportedly available and staff will help first-timers cook monja tableside, but this is a lived-in shitamachi joint mostly full of Japanese regulars rather than a tourist-oriented setup.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable this session — every request (direct curl and WebFetch) failed at the network/proxy level with a connection/403 error before returning any JSON, for both the full address and chōme-level fallback. This is a tooling access failure, not an empty geocode result. Address and relative location (Hoppy-dōri, Asakusa, near Sensō-ji) are well corroborated by multiple independent listings.
gf_detail: Monjayaki and okonomiyaki batter here is wheat-flour based by nature of the dish, and the dashi-based batter plus any tare/Worcestershire-style sauces used in Tokyo-style monjayaki typically contain wheat as well. No gluten-free batter substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-specific protocol is advertised anywhere for this shop. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00–23:00; Sat/Sun & holidays 10:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Tuesdays, though the closed day is not always fixed each week — call ahead.
neighborhood: Asakusa (Hoppy-dōri / Hoppy Street), Taitō City, Tokyo
seats_text: Roughly 50 seats total, including tatami floor seating and semi-private/private rooms for 15–25 guests.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and seafood toppings (beef tendon, pork, squid, shrimp) and egg-topped monja/okonomiyaki, and traditional Tokyo monjayaki batter is typically made with bonito (katsuobushi)-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian-specific menu items are advertised, so vegan diners should expect very limited to no safe options without extensive kitchen negotiation.

bio.origin: Traditional Tokyo shitamachi (old-downtown) monjayaki, learned hands-on rather than through formal training.
bio.background: The shop is run by a proprietress surnamed Tanabe who grew up helping out at monjayaki shops in Tokyo's shitamachi districts. She spent 16 years working in izakaya before running a monjayaki stall inside the Hanayashiki amusement park for nine years, building roughly 30 years of monjayaki experience before opening this Hisago-dori shop.
bio.specialty: Monjayaki made to the proprietress's own recipe with a wide range of toppings — from the signature seafood-and-pork 'Asakichi-ten' to tomato-cheese and mentaiko-mochi-cheese varieties — alongside okonomiyaki and teppanyaki dishes; staff will cook it for you or let you grill it yourself.
bio.philosophy: Keeps the traditional shitamachi method of making monjayaki while adapting recipes to guests' requests, serving it in a style suited to today's tastes.
roles: Proprietress (okami) — recipe development and hands-on monjayaki preparation

anecdotes.text: The okami's monjayaki background spans about 30 years: 16 years working in izakaya, followed by 9 years running a monjayaki stall inside the Hanayashiki amusement park, before she opened this shop on Hisago-dori.

caveats: Details are drawn from listing sites (Tabelog, Gurunavi/Rakuten Gurunavi, Hot Pepper Gourmet, and the Hisago-dori shopping street association page) rather than an independent official restaurant website, since no dedicated official site was found — the restaurant's Instagram account is the closest confirmed first-party presence. Coordinates could not be verified against Japan's GSI address-search geocoder because that host was blocked by network egress policy during verification (403), so lat/lng are null despite the location being corroborated as within the target area by multiple independent listings (Hisago-dori shopping street, ~2 min from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station Exit 1, close to Sensoji/Hanayashiki).
closed_days: Tuesdays (定休日), plus occasional irregular (不定休) closures — calling ahead is advisable.
cuisine: Monjayaki / Okonomiyaki (Teppanyaki)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional shitamachi monjayaki shop with no confirmed English menu, though it sits on Hisago-dori, a tourist-frequented shopping street near Sensoji, so simple ordering with pointing/photos is manageable even without Japanese.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by network egress policy (403) during verification, so precise coordinates could not be obtained programmatically. The address and location were nonetheless corroborated by multiple independent sources (Tabelog, Gurunavi, Hot Pepper, Hisago-dori shopping street association) as being on Hisago-dori in Asakusa 2-chome, about a 2-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station Exit 1 and close to Sensoji/Hanayashiki — consistent with being well within 0.8km of Sensoji, but lat/lng are left null rather than estimated.
gf_detail: Monjayaki batter is inherently wheat-flour-based, and the menu is built around traditional flour-and-dashi cooking on a shared teppan griddle. No gluten-free batter substitute, dedicated cooking surface, or wheat-free soy/Worcestershire sauce was found advertised by the restaurant or any listing. Cross-contact with wheat on the shared teppan is essentially unavoidable — not recommended for celiac or severe gluten-sensitive diners.
seats_text: Mix of counter, table, and 2nd-floor tatami (zashiki) seating; the tatami room reportedly seats up to about 12 and can be booked as a private room for groups.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork, squid, other seafood, cheese, and mentaiko (cod roe) toppings, and traditional monjayaki/okonomiyaki dashi is typically bonito (katsuo)-based. No vegan-specific items, dashi substitution, or vegan menu were found advertised by the restaurant or any listing — not a safe default for vegan diners without direct inquiry.

lead: 田中そば店 浅草店

roles: ramen/soba shop, part of the small Tanaka-Shoten ramen group
bio.origin: Adachi-ku, Tokyo (parent shop opened Dec 2000)
bio.background: The Asakusa branch opened on August 16, 2018, under the sign "中華そば専門 田中そば店" (Chūka-soba Specialist Tanaka Sobaten). It took over the storefront that had previously housed an Aomori-style niboshi ramen shop called "つし馬" (Tsushima). The parent company, Tanaka Shoten, opened its first shop in Adachi-ku in December 2000 and gradually expanded across Tokyo and beyond (Asakusa, Sendai, Akihabara, Misato — a 15th location opened in February 2021), always chasing the same simple goal: 'oishii' (delicious).
bio.specialty: Chūka soba (a niboshi/dried-sardine and shoyu based broth reminiscent of Kitakata-style ramen) and the Asakusa-exclusive Tsugaru Niboshi Ramen and Bari Niboshi Soba, which revive dishes from the shop's Tsushima-era predecessor.
bio.philosophy: The shop's guiding idea is 気軽に食べる田舎そば — 'unpretentious, countryside-style soba you can eat without fuss' — aiming for a nostalgic, almost salt-ramen-like flavor built on a niboshi-and-shoyu broth.

anecdotes.text: The Asakusa shop occupies the former location of the Aomori niboshi ramen shop Tsushima, and keeps two of that shop's dishes alive as an Asakusa-only menu: Tsugaru Niboshi Ramen and Bari Niboshi Soba.
Tanaka Shoten's company history traces from its December 2000 opening in Adachi-ku through expansion into Asakusa, Sendai, Akihabara and Misato, guided by a consistent focus on 'oishii'.

caveats: Two logistical points could not be pinned down precisely and diner-facing copy should hedge accordingly: (1) Hours — most listings (Navitime, Yahoo Map, Gnavi) say 11:00-21:00 every day with no closed days, but one aggregator reported a weekday split of 11:00-15:45/17:00-21:00 with straight 11:00-21:00 on weekends; this may reflect an older schedule. (2) Seat count — sources disagree, ranging from about 15 to 20 seats. (3) Exact GPS coordinates could not be verified: the GSI reverse-geocoding API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by network egress policy in this environment. The address and the repeatedly-cited '0-minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6, on the Kannon-dori arcade toward Sensoji' place it well inside the 0.8km Sensoji radius, but this is a geographic inference, not an API-confirmed geocode. No source discusses gluten-free/celiac or vegan accommodations specifically at this shop — it is a standard wheat-noodle, niboshi/shoyu-broth ramen shop with pork chāshū as a core topping, so GF/vegan diners should treat it as unsuitable rather than assume any hidden accommodation.
closed_days: None reported — most sources describe it as open year-round (定休日なし)
cuisine: ラーメン・そば（中華そば専門）
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering goes through a ticket vending machine (券売機) at the entrance with no table service to guide you, so being able to point at pictures/buttons and pay cash is enough — a little comfort with a Japanese-only setup helps, but no conversation is required.
gf_detail: This is a standard chūka-soba/ramen shop: the noodles are ordinary wheat-based chūka-men, not gluten-free, and the broth is niboshi (dried sardine) and shoyu based — soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat. No source mentions a gluten-free noodle substitution, a dedicated gluten-free prep area, or any allergen accommodation. Celiac and gluten-avoidant diners should treat this shop as unsuitable.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by network egress policy in this environment and could not be queried, so coordinates are not API-verified. Based on the confirmed address (東京都台東区浅草1-1-8) and multiple directory listings describing the shop as a 0-minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6 on the Kannon-dori arcade leading to Sensoji, the location is almost certainly well within 0.8km of the Sensoji reference point, but this is a geographic inference rather than a geocoded confirmation.
hours_raw: Most sources (Navitime, Yahoo Map, Gnavi-derived listings) state 11:00-21:00 every day, no closed days. One aggregator reported a different, possibly older schedule: weekdays 11:00-15:45 and 17:00-21:00 (split), weekends/holidays 11:00-21:00 straight through. Confirm current hours before visiting.
name_ja: 田中そば店 浅草店

vegan_detail: The broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) dashi with shoyu, and pork chāshū is a core topping across the menu. No source mentions any vegetarian or vegan broth/noodle option — fish- and meat-derived stock is central to essentially every dish, so vegan and vegetarian diners should treat this shop as unsuitable.

roles: Hokkaido specialty crab & seafood izakaya, operated by Marugoto Hokkaido Co., Ltd. (est. 2004), an Asakusa company running a cluster of Hokkaido-themed shops and restaurants near Kaminarimon
bio.background: Marugoto Hokkaido ("All of Hokkaido") started as an Asakusa antenna shop bringing Hokkaido specialty foods to Tokyo, and this Asakusa branch built an izakaya around that sourcing model: king crab (tarabagani), snow crab (zuwaigani) and hair crab (kegani) shipped in directly from Hokkaido and served in all-you-can-eat courses just off Kokusai-dori, a one-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — sister restaurant to the company's Jingisukan tavern and Hokkaido product shop nearby
bio.specialty: Three-crab all-you-can-eat course (king crab, snow crab, hair crab) served as sashimi, crab hot pot, grilled and fried crab, and finished with kani-zōsui (crab rice porridge); the restaurant recommends its house-made crab vinegar (自家製カニ酢) as a condiment for the crab.
bio.philosophy: A Hokkaido antenna-shop concept — deliver Hokkaido's crab and seafood directly to Asakusa at approachable prices rather than treating crab as a rare luxury.

anecdotes.text: A local Taito-ward feature piece calls crab "the king of winter flavors" and highlights being able to eat one's fill of it casually at this Asakusa shop.

bio.background: Originally a kanzashi (hair ornament) and Japanese craft-goods shop founded roughly 280 years ago near Sensoji Temple on Nakamise-dori — the shop name "Ginka" (銀花, "silver flower") is said to derive from the ornamental kanzashi it sold to geisha. After foot traffic collapsed during the COVID-19 pandemic, the shop was renewed in 2022 from a traditional crafts store into a fruit-and-wagashi sweets stall.
bio.specialty: Ichigo daifuku (strawberry mochi with red bean paste) and ichigo-kushi (fresh strawberry skewers)

anecdotes.text: The shop began as a kanzashi outfitter near Sensoji roughly 280 years ago; its name references the ornamental "silver flowers" (kanzashi) it originally sold.

The old crafts shop was reborn in 2022 as a fruit and wagashi sweets stand after pandemic-era foot traffic on Nakamise-dori dried up; it now sells strawberry skewers, cut melon, and strawberry daifuku.
A visiting blogger described the strawberry daifuku as filled with a plump, juicy strawberry paired with gently sweetened red bean paste and soft mochi.

caveats: This is a wagashi/fresh-fruit sweets stall, not a savory restaurant, so shoyu/dashi-style GF risks (tempura batter, soy sauce) don't apply — the live GF question is the starch used to dust the mochi and general cross-contact in prep, neither of which the shop has published. Two different Instagram handles (@ginkado_asakusa and @gingajp) surfaced in searches for what appears to be the same shop; which (if either) is the shop's own official account could not be confirmed, so official_url is left blank rather than guessed. Payment methods (cash-only or card-accepted) were not confirmed by any source. cultural_comfort_note: A walk-up sweets stand on Tokyo's most tourist-trodden street — you can order by pointing at the photo menu, but there's no confirmed English signage or English-speaking staff, so a little Japanese or gestures help, especially for allergy questions.

gf_detail: Daifuku mochi and red bean (anko) filling are naturally wheat-free by nature, but the shop has not published anything about the dusting starch used (potato starch vs. wheat flour), shared prep surfaces, or cross-contact with the custard/cream-based daifuku flavors. No dedicated gluten-free process has been confirmed — ask staff directly before eating if you have celiac disease.

seats_text: No table seating; there is a stand-up eat-in space beside the shop for takeaway items (eating while walking on Nakamise-dori itself is prohibited).
vegan_detail: Two of the five daifuku flavors (shio-koshi red bean and matcha) appear to be plant-based, since they're built on standard mochi and anko without cream; the other three (white peach whipped cream, custard, strawberry whipped cream) contain dairy and/or egg. The strawberry skewers (ichigo-kushi) are fresh fruit, typically with a sugar glaze. None of this has been explicitly confirmed as vegan by the shop itself, so ask staff before ordering.

bio.origin: Asakusa (opened 1974)
bio.background: Suzuyoshi has stood on Asakusa's Hoppy-dori since 1974 and is regularly cited as one of the street's most historic surviving taverns — a holdout of the postwar Showa-era drinking-street atmosphere as many neighboring bars have closed or been rebuilt amid the area's redevelopment around the Tokyo Skytree and the Olympics. A visiting food writer who first came roughly twenty years ago recalled the building still had an external staircase up to a residential unit above the bar, a detail that has since disappeared as the block modernized.
bio.specialty: Draft (nama/taru-nama) Hoppy — Suzuyoshi is widely cited as the only shop left on Hoppy-dori that still pours genuine draft Hoppy rather than the bottled version, served white, black, or half-and-half. It's paired with a spicy, Korean-influenced beef tendon stew (piri-kara gyusuji-ni) and a miso-based motsu-ni (offal stew), alongside grilled skewers, whelk simmered in soy, sashimi, and grilled fish.

anecdotes.text: 浅草ホッピー通りでも屈指の歴史を誇る『鈴芳』は、このお店でしか飲めないという樽生ホッピーやピリ辛テイストの韓国風牛すじ煮が人気です。

The author recalls a visit roughly 20 years earlier when the building still had an outdoor staircase leading to an upstairs residence — a vestige of Showa-era Hoppy-dori that has largely vanished as the surrounding blocks were rebuilt.

caveats: Geocoding could not be completed via the mandated GSI address-search API because outbound access to msearch.gsi.go.jp (and to tabelog.com, wikipedia, and most other general web hosts) was blocked by this session's network egress policy — every attempt returned a 403 at the proxy/CONNECT level, confirmed via the proxy's own status endpoint. lead: からあげ 縁 浅草総本店

roles: founder/operator: Arcland Service Holdings Co., Ltd.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (opened May 2010)
bio.background: Karaage Yukari's Asakusa store is the brand's original flagship ("sohonten"), opened in May 2010 as a takeout-only fried-chicken specialty counter. It grew from this single Asakusa shop into a nationwide chain of roughly 40 locations run by Arcland Service Holdings, the same company behind the "Karayama" karaage-set-meal chain.
bio.specialty: "Legend Karaage" (伝説のからあげ) — juicy chicken thigh marinated for a full day in a secret soy-sauce-based tare made without any garlic or ginger, then fried fresh to order until crisp and fragrant.
bio.philosophy: The shop is named 縁 ("Yukari," meaning bond/connection) to express the wish to cherish every encounter (ご縁) with a customer.

anecdotes.text: The marinade recipe reportedly took about two and a half years of trial and development before the shop opened, with the aim of creating karaage customers would one day call "legendary."

Unlike most karaage recipes, the tare deliberately leaves out garlic and ginger; the shop instead uses its own method for removing gaminess from the meat so the chicken's natural flavor comes through.

caveats: This is a takeout-only fried-chicken (karaage) counter chain, not a dietary-focused restaurant — it is included here only as a general reference point, not a gluten-free or vegan recommendation. Reported opening time varies slightly by source (10:00 vs 10:30); listed as 10:30 per the most recent sources. Coordinates could not be verified through the official GSI geocoder because that service was unreachable during this session (network/proxy outage); coordinates below are cross-confirmed instead via multiple independent map/POI listings for the same address.

The chicken is coated before frying and marinated overnight in a secret tare described as soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which contains wheat; all items are fried in shared equipment. Not suitable for a wheat/gluten allergy or celiac disease.

bio.philosophy: The concept is volume and clarity — generous meat portions served in a clean-tasting, slowly simmered pork clear broth, with an add-on 』たっぷりニク』 (extra meat) option for those who want even more.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened May 13, 2026 directly connected to the Ginza Line's Exit 6 in the Asakusa underground shopping street, a retro arcade setting near Kaminarimon.
The restaurant's calling card, as covered by local business news, is generous meat portions served in a pork clear (chintan) broth simmered slowly for a clean flavor.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — founded 1954, directly in front of Sensōji's Nitenmon (Two Deva Kings) Gate on the temple's east side
bio.background: Nitenmon Yabu is a long-established (老舗) soba shop that has stood at the Nitenmon Gate approach to Sensōji since 1954. The shop underwent a renovation (reported around 2017) that gave it a bright, modern concrete-and-glass exterior, though the interior is described as retaining the calm, unpretentious feel typical of an old Shitamachi (downtown) noodle shop.
bio.specialty: Katsudon (pork cutlet over rice) — among Asakusa's soba-shop-style rice bowls, local food writers repeat the saying that for tempura rice bowl (tendon) you go to Owariya, and for katsudon you go to Nitenmon Yabu. The menu otherwise covers a standard range of soba, udon, donburi and drinking snacks.

anecdotes.text: Among Asakusa's soba-shop rice bowls, the local saying goes: for tendon (tempura rice bowl) it's Owariya, for katsudon it's Nitenmon Yabu — a nod to how well-regarded this shop's pork cutlet bowl is among old-school Asakusa soba restaurants.

The shop sits just east of Sensōji, right in front of the Nitenmon Gate, and was founded in 1954; despite a modern renovation with bare concrete walls, stepping inside reveals the calm atmosphere typical of a downtown (shitamachi) soba shop.

caveats: Full source pages could not be directly fetched (JP dining-guide sites returned 403 to automated fetch), so details rely on search-result summaries of Tabelog, hotpepper, gnavi, retty, hitosara, cookdoor, nihon-soba.jp and soba-machichuka-1010.com listings; several of these disagree slightly on exact hours and closed days (some list irregular closures instead of a fixed weekly day off), so hours should be confirmed by phone (03-3841-8419) before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere; this appears to be a traditional, omnivore-focused neighborhood soba shop with no dietary-accommodation signaling. neighborhood: Asakusa (in front of Sensōji's Nitenmon Gate)

roles: confectionery maker (mamegashi / roasted bean & nut snacks)
bio.origin: Fukuyama, Hiroshima Prefecture
bio.background: Mametoku traces back to 1869 (Meiji 2), when the Tokunaga family began in Fukuyama, Hiroshima as a grain and dried-goods merchant. The business shifted early on to focus on roasted bean snacks, and the shop took the name "Mametoku" (豆徳), combining mame (beans) with the Tokunaga family name of its founder, Tokunaga Shintarō. The Asakusa branch is one of several Mametoku locations run by the parent company, Tokunaga Seika Co., Ltd., and opened on December 21, 2020, in the Asakusa Shin-Nakamise shopping arcade, about a 3-minute walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon (Thunder Gate).
bio.specialty: Takezumi Mame (bamboo-charcoal-coated beans), developed in 2002, is the brand's longtime signature item. The Asakusa store additionally sells an exclusive item called "MAMERON BAR," a stick-shaped wafer topped with colorful roasted nuts.
bio.philosophy: The company describes finishing its bean and nut confections by hand rather than relying solely on automated production, aiming to offer simple, everyday bean-based treats as part of a healthy diet, while continually developing new flavors alongside its traditional recipes.

anecdotes.text: 豆徳浅草店opened December 21, 2020, roughly a 3-minute walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon (Raijin) Gate, inside the Asakusa Shin-Nakamise arcade, and is known locally for an Asakusa-exclusive item, the colorful nut-topped "MAMERON BAR."

The Mametoku name and business history date to 1869 in Fukuyama, Hiroshima, where the company (Tokunaga Seika Co., Ltd.) began as a grain merchant before specializing in bean confections; the shop name derives from founder Tokunaga Shintarō's surname combined with "mame" (beans).

This is a bean/nut confectionery retail shop rather than a sit-down restaurant — no eat-in seating was found in any source, and no gluten-free or vegan certification/labeling was found, so GF and vegan status are marked "ask" rather than confirmed.

cultural_comfort_note: It's a simple walk-up snack counter in a tourist-heavy shopping arcade — pointing at packaged bean and nut snacks and paying works fine even without Japanese, though staff may not speak much English.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer, or celiac-specific information was found in any source. As a traditional mamegashi (bean/nut confectionery) shop, some products are likely flavored or coated with soy sauce (shōyu, which in Japan typically contains wheat) or other wheat-based seasonings, and shared equipment across the 80+ item lineup is likely. Plain roasted beans may be lower-risk, but nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without asking staff and checking individual package ingredient labels — not recommended for celiac-level strictness without on-site verification.
hours_raw: 10:00–18:00, no fixed closed days (年中無休)
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session due to network egress restrictions, so coordinates could not be programmatically confirmed. Proximity to Sensōji is nonetheless well established from text sources: the address (東京都台東区浅草1-29-4) places it in Asakusa 1-chōme, and multiple independent sources (official Mametoku site, Asakusa Shin-Nakamise shopping-street site, Asakusa local guides) describe the shop as roughly a 3-minute walk from Sensōji's Kaminarimon (Thunder) Gate — comfortably inside the 0.8 km Sensōji radius.
neighborhood: 浅草（東武・都営・メトロ）／浅草新仲見世商店街, near Sensō-ji Kaminarimon
seats_text: No seating or eat-in area was found in any source — appears to be a retail snack/confectionery counter for takeaway purchase only.
vegan_detail: No vegan certification or labeling was found. Many plain roasted bean and nut items are inherently plant-based, but the broader 80+ item lineup (including sweetened, chocolate-coated, or novelty items like the MAMERON BAR) may contain honey, dairy (milk chocolate), or other animal-derived ingredients, and traditional Japanese confections can hide bonito/katsuo-derived seasonings. Vegans should check individual ingredient labels or ask staff per item rather than assume the whole product line is vegan.

bio.origin: Opened 2013, Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Half-grilled quail (uzura), plus charcoal-grilled French quail and pigeon and Amagi black chicken from Izu
bio.philosophy: An unassuming, café-like storefront hiding a serious commitment to sourcing — prized domestic poultry breeds, imported French quail and pigeon for variety, and seasonal vegetables — combined with technique picked up at a French-restaurant-run yakitori spinoff.
bio.background: Opened in 2013 down a quiet residential backstreet of Asakusa's Kannon-ura district, ちゃこーる (Chacole) is a small charcoal-grilled yakitori counter run by chef-owner Hisako Takahashi, known by the nickname "Chako." She trained as a grill hand at Mantori (萬鳥), an inventive yakitori offshoot launched by the French restaurant La Chèvre — a lineage food writers say still shows in her style.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name blends the English word "charcoal" with the owner's nickname, Chako.

Takahashi trained as a grill hand at Mantori (萬鳥), a yakitori shop created by the French restaurant La Chèvre — a background the food magazine dancyu highlighted as giving her yakitori a distinct French inflection.
The signature half-grilled quail is described as a dish that wins over essentially every guest who tries it.

caveats: Small, reservation-oriented backstreet yakitori counter; the shop is known to close for the evening if no phone bookings (in Japanese) come in by mid-afternoon, so calling ahead is effectively required. No English menu, gluten-free protocol, or vegan menu has been documented — include this listing for its local/cultural character rather than dietary accessibility. Payment method (cash-only or cards accepted) could not be confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: Wednesdays (定休日：水曜日)
cuisine: やきとり・焼き鳥 (charcoal-grilled yakitori)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, locals'-backstreet counter shop with phone-only reservations in Japanese and no evidence of an English menu — best suited to diners comfortable navigating in Japanese or with a local guide/translation help.
geocode_note: The GSI address geocoder (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and several map-data sites were unreachable during verification (network/proxy connectivity failures affected essentially all outbound fetches in this session, including unrelated hosts). Coordinates given are an area-level estimate for Asakusa 4-chome based on known Asakusa street geography (this address sits roughly 400-500m from Sensoji, well inside the 0.8km Asakusa anchor radius), not a precise address-level geocode.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, options, or protocol are documented anywhere in available sources. As a traditional charcoal-grilled yakitori restaurant, the tare glazing sauce almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce, and even salt-grilled (shio) skewers are cooked on the same shared charcoal grill as sauce-glazed items, creating a real cross-contact risk. Not recommended for strict celiac/gluten-free needs without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shio (salt) and tare (sauce) skewers are cooked on the same shared charcoal grill; no separation between wheat-sauce-glazed and plain items is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare glazing sauce used on charcoal-grilled yakitori is, per standard preparation, soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat; no gluten-free tare alternative is documented for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Any vegetable skewers would be grilled on the same charcoal grill used for chicken, quail, and pigeon.

seats_text: Small counter-style yakitori shop in a converted residential building; exact seat count not published in available sources.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakitori restaurant built around specialty poultry, quail, and pigeon; no vegan menu, dish, or accommodation has been documented. Seasonal vegetable skewers may be offered but would be grilled on the same shared charcoal grill as meat, and no confirmation exists that broths or sides avoid bonito-based dashi. Not recommended for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.

bio.origin: Before it was 紅鶴, this counter was a kissaten (old-style coffee shop) called "フラミンゴ" (Flamingo), run by the current owner's family in Asakusa for more than 50 years. In 2017 the owner, Makita-san, renewed the shop and renamed it 紅鶴 — which is simply the Japanese kanji word for "flamingo" — carrying the old shop's identity into its new pancake-focused form.
bio.specialty: Bacon and Fried Egg Pancake (ベーコンと目玉焼きのパンケーキ) — a three-layer soufflé-style rice-flour pancake stacked with honey, marmalade and bacon, topped with a fried egg and a generous pour of hollandaise sauce.
bio.philosophy: Makita-san builds the pancakes around rice flour and kibi (raw cane) sugar instead of wheat flour and granulated sugar, and tops them with honey rather than maple syrup, aiming for a lighter batter that doesn't sit heavy in the stomach — his stated goal is a pancake that men, older customers and children can all enjoy comfortably, not just a dessert-cafe crowd.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name and premise trace directly back to the owner's parents' decades-old kissaten "Flamingo," which he reopened as 紅鶴 (its kanji equivalent) in 2017 when he pivoted the menu to rice-flour pancakes.

The cafe drew a wave of new attention after being featured on the TV program 沸騰ワード10 in September 2025, with reports (and rumor) that Justin Bieber had eaten there alongside a long list of visiting Japanese celebrities.

caveats: Extremely popular and hard to get into: same-day numbered queue tickets (整理券) are typically distributed very early (reports range from roughly 7:00 to 8:30am for a 10am opening), with no phone or online reservations; some visitors report a ¥1,000-per-person deposit taken when the ticket is issued. The kitchen commonly sells out and closes before its nominal end time. Reported closed days are inconsistent across sources (mostly described as irregular/不定休, though one source names Wednesday specifically) — check their Instagram (@benizuru_homemade_pancake) shortly before visiting. Seat counts also vary by source (roughly 8-14 counter seats).
closed_days: Reported as irregular (不定休) by most sources; one blog specifically names Wednesday as the closed day, which conflicts with others describing it as open that day — unconfirmed, verify via their Instagram before visiting.
cuisine: Café / Gluten-free Rice Flour Pancakes
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu was confirmed and there is no phone/online booking — you must queue in person for a same-day numbered ticket, a distinctly Japanese-style system that is nonetheless well documented in English-language travel blogs and manageable if you arrive early and follow the queue etiquette.
geocode_note: The official GSI geocoding API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and fallback geocoders (geocoding.jp, Google Maps, Nominatim) were all unreachable from this environment (network/proxy policy blocked the connections), so no verified coordinates could be obtained. The confirmed street address (西浅草2-1-11, Taito-ku) and its documented 2-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station place it well within the Sensōji target radius, but precise lat/lng could not be independently geocoded this session.
gf_detail: The signature pancakes are made with rice flour (米粉) and kibi sugar instead of wheat flour and granulated sugar — this is the shop's core differentiator and is confirmed across many independent sources. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, equipment, or wheat-free certification was found in any source; the menu also includes items such as waffles whose flour was not confirmed wheat-free, and sauces/toppings (hollandaise, marmalade) have unconfirmed ingredients. Treat this as "rice-flour-forward" rather than allergy-certified, and confirm cross-contact directly with staff if you have celiac disease.
hours_raw: Reported hours vary by source: some list 9:30-17:00, others 10:00-17:00, with same-day numbered queue tickets (整理券) distributed as early as 7:00-8:30am for the roughly 10am opening. The kitchen commonly sells out and closes before the nominal end time. Closed days are described as irregular (不定休) by most sources, though one blog specifies Wednesdays — unconfirmed.
neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa, 2 min walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, near Kokusai-dōri

positives.text: The pancake batter itself is made with rice flour and kibi (raw cane) sugar rather than wheat flour and granulated sugar — described by the owner as a deliberate choice to make a lighter pancake that's easier to digest.

seats_text: Counter seating only; reported seat counts vary across sources, from roughly 8 to 14 seats.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly: the signature dishes are built around bacon, fried eggs, hollandaise sauce (butter and egg yolk based), and honey as the standard sweetener across the pancake menu. No vegan alternative, plant-based substitution, or vegan-labeled item was found in any source.

bio.origin: Taiwan (the owner grew up in Taiwan)
bio.background: Owner Haruka Iwahashi, who grew up in Taiwan, founded a company focused on Oriental vegetarian cooking in April 2019, then opened Shochiku-en Cafe in Nishi-Asakusa near Kappabashi that December. She wanted to share the appeal of natural, additive-free plant-based cooking — and her own Taiwanese home cooking — with more people in Japan.
bio.specialty: Vegan Taiwanese luròu fàn (braised-'pork' rice bowl), the cafe's most popular dish, made plant-based in place of the pork used in the traditional version
bio.philosophy: To convey the wonder of natural food and vegan cuisine to as many people in Japan as possible, using carefully selected organic, domestic ingredients in a kitchen that excludes both animal products and wheat entirely.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's signature dish reimagines luròu fàn, the Taiwanese braised pork rice bowl the owner grew up eating, recreated with plant-based ingredients instead of meat.
Iwahashi opened an Oriental-vegetarian company in April 2019 before opening Shochiku-en Cafe in Asakusa that December, aiming to spread appreciation of natural/vegan food in Japan.

caveats: Hours and phone number vary slightly across directory listings (older sources show 9:30-16:30/17:00; more recent, consistent sources show the 11:00-20:00 / 11:00-15:00 pattern used here) — worth a quick call-ahead check. Exact seat count is inconsistently reported (very small space, roughly a handful of counter seats plus two small tables). Precise coordinates could not be confirmed via the official GSI geocoding API (blocked in this environment) and are triangulated instead from cited walking distances to Tawaramachi and Asakusa (TX) stations.
closed_days: None reported (open daily; shortened hours Wed & Thu)
cuisine: Vegan cafe with Taiwanese/Asian-fusion dishes, entirely gluten-free kitchen
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, Japanese-run neighborhood cafe near Kappabashi's kitchenware street — menu and staff are primarily Japanese-oriented, but the shop is well covered by vegan/gluten-free guide sites and used to visitors seeking plant-based food, so simple English or pointing at photos should get you through.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) could not be reached from this environment (network access blocked). Coordinates below are a manual estimate triangulated from the cafe's cited walking distances (about 6-7 minutes) from both Tawaramachi Station (Ginza Line) and Asakusa Station (Tsukuba Express), in the Nishi-Asakusa/Kappabashi area — treat as approximate (±150m), not a verified geocode.
gf_detail: The cafe states its entire kitchen is gluten-free and every menu item is gluten-free — no wheat is used anywhere in the food. No specific public statement was found confirming whether a dedicated fryer is used for fried items (e.g., the cafe's pizza) or how soy sauce/seasonings are sourced, though the "completely gluten-free kitchen" claim implies wheat-based soy sauce is avoided by design.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun & holidays: 11:00-20:00 (food L.O. 19:30, drink L.O. 19:30); Wed, Thu: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30, drink L.O. 14:30)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The cafe describes its kitchen as completely gluten-free, with no wheat brought into the kitchen at all — every menu item is gluten-free by design rather than by substitution.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cafe is 100% plant-based across its entire menu and holds vegan certification from NPO Vege Project Japan, with no animal-derived ingredients (including dairy, egg, honey) used anywhere.

positives.text: Because every dish on the menu is both vegan and gluten-free, diners don't need to cross-check individual items for hidden animal or wheat ingredients.
The owner built the cafe specifically around natural, additive-free vegan cooking, using carefully selected organic and domestic ingredients.

seats_text: Very small space: a few counter seats plus two small tables (reports of size vary, roughly 8-10 seats total)
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based menu, vegan-certified by NPO Vege Project Japan; no dashi, katsuobushi, egg, dairy, or honey. Signature dish is a vegan version of Taiwanese luròu fàn (braised "pork" rice); other dishes include vegan sweet-and-sour "pork," a mapo tofu bowl, and gapao rice, plus vegan/gluten-free cakes and a chocolate sandwich made with organic ingredients.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-32-11 九十一ビル2F

bio.origin: National restaurant chain operated by Reins International (part of the Colowide group) — this is one branch (Asakusa) of the nationwide "Onyasai" vegetable shabu-shabu chain, not an independent or chef-owned establishment.
bio.background: Onyasai built its identity around vegetable-forward shabu-shabu: an all-you-can-eat format pairing thinly sliced meats with a large rotating selection of domestic vegetables, centered on a lineup of proprietary house-made dashi broths rather than a single generic soup base.
bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat vegetable and meat shabu-shabu with a choice of signature broths, most notably the "Sandaime Kiwami Dashi" (三代目極みだし), a seafood-based broth blending sardine, soda-bonito and mackerel with a white soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: For its 25th anniversary, the chain added two new proprietary dashi options to its grand menu — "Umi no Megumi Dashi" (a citrus-and-seafood broth) and "Awa-iro Ushio Dashi" (a sea bream and sea-salt broth) — continuing its broth-first approach to vegetable shabu-shabu.
The chain's signature broth, Sandaime Kiwami Dashi, is built from a fish-stock base of iwashi (sardine), soda-katsuo and saba (mackerel) finished with a premium white soy sauce.

caveats: This is a national chain branch (Reins International/Colowide group), not an independent restaurant — included here as a mainstream vegetable-shabu-shabu option, not a GF/vegan specialist. Hours are consistent across two independently-aggregated sources (11:30-15:00, 16:00-23:00 daily, no regular closed day), but one weaker secondary source suggested weekday-lunch-closed; the daily-open version is treated as authoritative. WebFetch and direct HTTPS access to Tabelog, the official onyasai.com store page, and the GSI (国土地理院) geocoding API were all blocked by this session's outbound network/proxy policy (confirmed 403 "policy denial" via the proxy status endpoint for msearch.gsi.go.jp, tabelog.com, onyasai.com, and even www.google.com), so all facts here come from WebSearch result synthesis of those pages rather than direct page fetches, and coordinates could not be machine-geocoded — see geocode_note.
closed_days: 年中無休 (open year-round, no regular weekly closed day per aggregated listings)
cuisine: 野菜しゃぶしゃぶ・野菜鍋
cultural_comfort_note: A mainstream Japanese chain restaurant with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its central Asakusa location (2 minutes from Asakusa Station) and an English Tabelog listing suggest some familiarity with tourists.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or accommodation was found. The house dashi broths are the medium everything is cooked in, and the signature "Sandaime Kiwami Dashi" is explicitly built with soy sauce (a wheat-based white soy sauce), so wheat is present in the shared cooking liquid itself, not just in a separate dipping sauce — meaning even plain meat/vegetables cooked in it pick up gluten. A chain-wide allergen reference sheet is reportedly available on request, but shared pots and cooking equipment mean cross-contact is essentially unavoidable in the all-you-can-eat format. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, specific confirmation from staff.
geocode_note: Could not be machine-geocoded: the GSI (国土地理院) address-search API and direct fetches to Tabelog/onyasai.com were all blocked by this session's outbound proxy with 403 "policy denial" (confirmed via the proxy's own status diagnostics, which also showed www.google.com and nominatim.openstreetmap.org being rejected — a session-wide egress restriction, not specific to this address). Address (浅草1-32-11) is corroborated by multiple listings as a 2-minute walk from Asakusa Station (Ginza Line, Exit 6), which places it well within the Sensō-ji/Asakusa 0.8km target radius, but precise lat/lng coordinates are not available this session.
name_en: Shabu-shabu On'yasai Asakusa-ten (Shabu Shabu Onyasai Asakusa)
name_ja: しゃぶしゃぶ温野菜 浅草店

gf_cross_contamination.text: The all-you-can-eat shabu-shabu format uses shared pots and cooking/washing equipment across meat and vegetable items, creating cross-contact risk for allergen-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chain's signature "Sandaime Kiwami Dashi" broth is built around a white soy sauce blended with sardine, soda-bonito and mackerel stock — soy sauce (and thus wheat) is in the base cooking liquid itself, not only in a side dipping sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The default/signature broths are fish-stock based (sardine, soda-katsuo, mackerel), and even the soy-milk broth option is cut with bonito and kombu dashi, so no broth on the standard menu is confirmed vegan; the restaurant's core product is all-you-can-eat meat shabu-shabu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff can reportedly provide a printed 7-major-allergen reference sheet on request, but the chain notes shared cooking/washing equipment means cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.

positives.text: The chain is built around a large, frequently-rotating selection of domestic (国産) vegetables served alongside meat in an all-you-can-eat format, with several distinct proprietary broths to choose from.

vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The core menu is all-you-can-eat meat and vegetable shabu-shabu; the named default broths are fish-stock based (sardine, soda-bonito, mackerel), and even the soy-milk broth is finished with bonito and kombu dashi. A plain kombu (kelp-only) broth reportedly exists among the broth choices, which could in principle avoid animal-derived dashi, but no confirmed fully plant-based tare/dipping sauce or vegan protocol was found — a vegan diner would need to ask in detail and likely couldn't rely on any of the signature broths.

lead: 黒毛和牛 壱乃屋 浅草
name_ja: 黒毛和牛 壱乃屋 浅草
neighborhood: Asakusa (near Kaminarimon Gate, 浅草駅徒歩1〜3分)
cuisine: 和牛ステーキ（黒毛和牛レアステーキ重専門店）
vegan_detail: This is a single-specialty wagyu beef restaurant built entirely around a rare-steak rice bowl finished with an egg yolk and a beef-based dashi broth (hitsumabushi-style pour). No vegetarian or vegan preparation, substitute, or plant-based version of the signature dish was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
gf_detail: The dish's signature "warishita" sauce is homemade from soy sauce (shoyu) and mirin — both typically contain wheat — and no gluten-free sauce alternative, dedicated fryer, or allergen-free protocol is mentioned anywhere. Because the menu is essentially one composed dish built on this sauce, there is no visible way to avoid wheat exposure. Treat as not safe for celiac/gluten-free diners without a direct, in-person conversation with staff (and even then, cross-contact risk in a tiny single-dish kitchen is likely high).
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-34-6 大阪屋ビル1F
geocode_note: Could not geocode: the GSI address-search endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked at the network egress/proxy level for this session (policy denial on CONNECT, not a site-side error), and no alternate geocoder (Nominatim, Yahoo Map page) was reachable either — all were blocked the same way. Multiple independent listings (Tabelog, Rakuten Gurunavi, EAT Tokyo/menu-tokyo.jp) consistently describe the address as 台東区浅草1-34-6, roughly a 1–3 minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6 and adjacent to Kaminarimon Gate, which places it well inside Asakusa 1-chome — strongly suggesting it falls within the 0.8km Sensōji radius — but this could not be confirmed with actual coordinates.
seats_text: Small, compact shop (one secondary source cites about 16 seats, but this could not be independently confirmed).
closed_days: None listed — open every day of the year (年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: Menu and service are Japanese-first with a queue-based, single-dish walk-in format typical of a small Asakusa specialty shop; expect to point/order simply rather than have detailed English allergy conversations.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — opened December 2022
bio.background: The shop's owner trained for eight years at a restaurant with roughly 260 years of history in Nihonbashi Ningyōchō before striking out to open their own place in Asakusa, wanting to share Japanese food culture and tradition through a single dish.
bio.specialty: 黒毛和牛レアステーキ重 (Premium Rare Wagyu Steak Rice Bowl) — thin-sliced rare wagyu over rice, described as 'three flavors in one meal'.
bio.philosophy: Positioned as an evolution of Edo-period cuisine reimagined as a modern rice-bowl format, aiming to carry Japanese culinary tradition from Asakusa out into the world.

anecdotes.text: Diners are guided through a three-stage way of eating the bowl: first plain to taste the meat and sauce, then mixed with a rich egg yolk, and finally finished hitsumabushi-style by pouring a beef dashi broth with condiments over the rice.
The shop has drawn continuous daily lines since its December 2022 opening and has been featured on Japanese gourmet TV (e.g. Hirunandesu!, Oct 2024).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house 'warishita' sauce is homemade from soy sauce and mirin — no gluten-free or wheat-free version is mentioned in any source found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu centers on wagyu beef, egg yolk, and a beef-based dashi broth; no vegan, vegetarian, or plant-based option was found in any source.

caveats: Small single-specialty shop with essentially one core dish (wagyu rare-steak rice bowl) — no vegan/vegetarian/gluten-free accommodation was found in any source, so this should be flagged as OMNI-risk despite MOM_AND_POP categorization (chosen for its small, independent, chef-driven character). Exact GPS coordinates could not be verified because the required GSI geocoding endpoint (and fallback geocoders) were blocked by network egress policy in this session — address and walking-distance-from-landmark claims are corroborated by multiple listing sites but not independently pinpointed. Seat count (~16) is from a single secondary source and unconfirmed. No official restaurant website was found, only an Instagram account (@ichinoya.wagyu) and third-party listing pages; several primary listing pages (Tabelog itself, Gurunavi, Hitosara, Hamoni, Yahoo Map) returned 403/blocked responses when fetched directly, so facts were sourced via search-engine snippets of those pages rather than direct page fetches.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-43-8 原田ビル2F

bio.origin: Thailand
bio.background: JIMJUM is run single-handedly by a Thai proprietress (referred to by regulars as "mama"), who cooks each dish herself in a small, home-style setting on the second floor of a building near Asakusa Station.
bio.specialty: JIMJUM nabe — a Thai-style shabu-shabu hot pot built on a tom yum goong broth, packed with herbs, chicken, seafood and vegetables, marketed as a healthy, detoxifying dish.
bio.philosophy: An unpretentious pursuit of authentic, home-style Thai cooking — welcoming guests the way you'd welcome a friend into your home, and trying to keep prices reasonable for regulars even as food costs rise.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has been ranked No.1 in the Thai Curry category of Japan's Curry Database for three consecutive years.
It's run by a single Thai proprietress working alone, described by visitors as a cozy, home-style Thai spot where "everything you eat is delicious" despite the minimal, small setting.
Even amid rising food prices, the restaurant has been noted for holding the line on its budget-friendly lunch pricing (lunches under 1,000 yen).

caveats: Could not independently fetch the Tabelog page or run the GSI geocoder directly this session (outbound fetch tool returned 403 for tabelog.com, jimjum.net, and msearch.gsi.go.jp on every attempt) — all facts below come from search-result snippets of those and other pages (jimjum.net, Hot Pepper Gourmet, blogs), not a direct page fetch, so treat address/hours as reasonably but not fully verified. Coordinates could not be produced (geocoder unreachable) — left null. Multiple independent sources describe the location as "1-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station exit A1" and "in the very center of Asakusa," which is consistent with the required proximity to Sensōji, but this was not confirmed with hard coordinates. Payment methods, exact seat count, and reservation policy were not confirmed by any source and are left conservative/unconfirmed. Two Tabelog listings exist for this restaurant name in the same district (13149866, the target ID with no relocation tag, and 13197978 tagged 【移転】); based on which one carries the un-tagged title and June 2026 reviews, 13149866 (the target URL) appears to be the currently active listing, but this could not be double-checked by direct page fetch.
closed_days: Wednesdays (水曜定休)
cuisine: タイ料理 (Thai)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, one-woman-run neighborhood Thai spot in the heart of Asakusa — simple, friendly interactions are easy, but don't expect an English menu or extensive English-speaking staff.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free process, allergy menu, or dedicated fryer was found in any source. Thai cooking here likely relies on soy sauce and other seasonings that can contain wheat, and dishes are cooked in a small home-style kitchen with shared equipment. Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering — nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without confirmation.
geocode_note: GSI address geocoder (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable this session (network/proxy returned 403 on every attempt), so coordinates could not be generated and are left null rather than invented. Secondary sources place the restaurant "1 minute on foot from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station exit A1" and describe it as being in the center of Asakusa, which is consistent with — but not proof of — falling inside the 0.8km Sensōji radius required for this app.
neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa) — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station
seats_text: Small, cozy seating on the 2nd floor of the building; exact seat count not confirmed by any source.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. The restaurant's signature hot pot and many other dishes contain chicken and seafood, and Thai cooking commonly uses fish sauce and oyster sauce. Vegans/vegetarians should ask the staff directly about vegetable-only options and hidden animal-derived seasonings (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste) before ordering — nothing here is confirmed vegan-safe.

lead: わらび餅もとこ 浅草店

roles: Warabi mochi specialty shop (branch of the Sugamo original)

anecdotes.text: The original わらび餅もとこ shop has been a beloved fixture in Sugamo, Tokyo for over 15 years; the Asakusa branch opened in December 2021 on the Nishi-Sandō approach street just west of Sensō-ji, and quickly became known for lines of customers.

The shop is credited with originating the 'warabi mochi milk tea' drink — chewy warabi-mochi pearls served in a milk tea (matcha, hōjicha, or black tea base, plus seasonal fruit punch versions) — alongside boat-shaped (fune-gata) fresh warabi mochi sold for eating while strolling Nishi-Sandō.
As of mid-2026 the Asakusa shop is operating from a temporary spot about one minute's walk from its usual address while its original location undergoes renovation.

bio.background: Branch of a long-running Sugamo warabi-mochi specialty shop; opened its Asakusa location in December 2021 on the Nishi-Sandō street leading toward Sensō-ji.
bio.origin: Sugamo, Tokyo (original shop, 15+ years)
bio.specialty: Warabi mochi milk tea (their original creation) and fresh boat-shaped warabi mochi for eating while walking
